proposals.title: Beyond Germanium: A Systematic Investigation of Sn- and Si-Based Capping Layers for Controlled Grain Growth and Defect Suppression in CZTSSe Absorbers
proposals.motivation: Kesterite CZTSSe solar cells suffer from low open-circuit voltage, largely due to fine-grained layers and defect clusters at the rear interface, which are linked to premature nucleation during selenization. While Ge capping has shown promise in suppressing this nucleation and enhancing grain growth, its high cost and potential for volatile GeSe_x loss present significant barriers to commercialization. Exploring alternative, earth-abundant capping materials is crucial for developing a scalable, high-efficiency kesterite technology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that capping layers based on Sn or Si can provide superior thermodynamic and kinetic control over the selenization reaction compared to Ge, leading to larger, more uniform grains and a reduction in detrimental Sn-related defects (e.g., Sn_Sn_x) at the CZTSSe/Mo back interface.
proposals.method: We will deposit thin (<50 nm) metallic Sn and amorphous Si capping layers via sputtering onto precursor stacks. A systematic selenization study will be conducted using rapid thermal processing with controlled Se vapor pressure and temperature ramps (300-550°C). The resulting films will be characterized by in-situ XRD to monitor phase evolution, cross-sectional SEM/EBSD for grain structure, and Raman spectroscopy for secondary phases. Advanced atom-scale analysis via atom probe tomography (APT) and scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM-EDS) will be employed to quantify elemental distribution and identify point defects at the critical back interface region.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a materials library for back-contact engineering in CZTSSe, directly linking capping layer chemistry to grain growth dynamics and defect formation. It will provide fundamental insights into the role of capping layer reactivity with Se and precursor elements, moving beyond the empirical use of Ge. The findings could identify a lower-cost pathway to >13% efficient kesterite solar cells by improving back-contact quality.
proposals.novelty: While Ge capping is an active research area, a direct, atomically-resolved comparative study of Sn and Si as alternative capping agents has not been performed. The novelty lies in using APT and in-situ XRD to deconvolute the specific mechanisms—such as the formation of transient liquid phases or diffusion barriers—by which these elements influence nucleation kinetics and defect chemistry.

proposals.title: Extending the Ge Cap Layer Strategy: A Universal Phase Evolution Intervention for Grain Growth in Kesterite and Perovskite Thin-Film Photovoltaics
proposals.motivation: The efficiency of thin-film solar cells like CZTSSe and metal halide perovskites is often limited by poor crystallization quality, including small grains, voids, and secondary phases, which increase non-radiative recombination. Recent pre-2026 work by Green's group demonstrated that a Ge cap layer during annealing dramatically improves CZTSSe grain growth and device performance. This suggests a potentially universal materials processing strategy that has not been systematically explored beyond kesterites.
proposals.hypothesis: The Ge cap layer strategy, which functions by modifying the local chemical potential and suppressing volatile loss during annealing, can be successfully transferred to other compound semiconductor thin films, specifically lead-tin (Pb-Sn) mixed perovskites and pure-sulfide CZTS, to engineer their phase evolution and enhance grain growth.
proposals.method: We will fabricate Pb-Sn perovskite and CZTS precursor thin films via spin-coating and sputtering, respectively. A thin, evaporated Ge layer (5-20 nm) will be deposited on selected samples prior to annealing in a controlled atmosphere. Advanced micro-to-atom scale characterizations (pre-2026 state-of-the-art) will be employed: in-situ grazing-incidence X-ray diffraction (GIXRD) to monitor real-time phase evolution, cross-sectional SEM/EBSD for grain size and orientation analysis, and XPS/TOF-SIMS for elemental distribution and interdiffusion studies. Device performance will be correlated with structural changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish whether the Ge cap intervention is a generalizable materials processing principle for thin-film photovoltaics. It will provide fundamental insights into the role of external metallic layers in controlling the kinetics of crystallization and phase formation. Successful application could lead to new, simple processing routes to achieve high-quality, large-grain absorber layers for higher-efficiency, stable solar cells.
proposals.novelty: While Ge doping and alloying in CZTSSe is well-studied, using a Ge cap as a transient, sacrificial layer to manipulate crystallization is a novel concept for perovskites and pure-sulfide CZTS. Extending this specific intervention strategy from kesterites to chemically distinct perovskite systems is a non-obvious and high-risk, high-reward exploration.

proposals.title: Systematic Optimization of Germanium Cap Layer Deposition for Enhanced Grain Growth and Efficiency in CZTSSe Solar Cells
proposals.motivation: Kesterite CZTSSe solar cells are promising thin-film photovoltaic absorbers, but their efficiency is limited by high open-circuit voltage deficits, often linked to small grains and high defect densities at grain boundaries. Recent pre-2026 work, including by Green's group, shows that Ge incorporation, particularly via a Ge cap layer, can significantly enhance grain growth and carrier collection. However, the precise relationship between the Ge cap's physical deposition parameters, its resultant thickness/morphology, and the final grain structure remains empirically underexplored, hindering reproducible high-efficiency device fabrication.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an optimal window exists for Ge cap layer thickness and deposition method (e.g., thermal evaporation vs. sputtering) which maximizes liquid-phase-assisted grain growth during selenization, leading to larger, more uniform CZTSSe grains with reduced bulk and interfacial recombination, thereby improving device efficiency.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a series of CZTS precursor stacks (Mo/SLGS/CZTS) with Ge cap layers deposited by thermal evaporation and RF magnetron sputtering, systematically varying thickness from 0 to 150 nm in precise steps. All samples will undergo identical rapid thermal processing selenization. The primary characterization will combine scanning electron microscopy for grain size statistics, X-ray diffraction for phase and strain analysis, and secondary ion mass spectrometry for Ge depth profiling. Completed solar cells will be evaluated using current-voltage and external quantum efficiency measurements to correlate structural properties with photovoltaic parameters, specifically focusing on open-circuit voltage and fill factor.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative processing-structure-property relationship for Ge cap layers in CZTSSe, providing a clear recipe for optimal grain growth enhancement. It will contribute directly to the ongoing effort to push CZTSSe efficiencies closer to the practical limits recorded in the Solar Cell Efficiency Tables, offering a targeted pathway to reduce the voltage deficit through controlled microstructural engineering.
proposals.novelty: While Ge's beneficial role is known, this study uniquely decouples and systematically maps the independent effects of cap layer deposition technique and precise thickness on the grain growth mechanism and final device performance, moving beyond binary Ge presence/absence studies to establish a detailed process optimization framework.

proposals.title: Decoupling Recombination Pathways in CZTSSe: The Role of Deferred Crystallization on Point Defects and Heterointerface Quality
proposals.motivation: Kesterite CZTSSe solar cells suffer from a large open-circuit voltage deficit, primarily attributed to non-radiative recombination. While recent work, including that by Green's group, has shown that grain boundary passivation and liquid-phase-assisted grain growth can improve carrier collection, the specific impact of crystallization kinetics on point defect formation and the critical absorber/CdS interface remains unclear. Understanding and controlling these specific recombination pathways is essential to push efficiencies beyond the current ~13% barrier and approach the material's theoretical potential.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully controlled, deferred crystallization process during the selenization of solution-processed CZTSSe precursors will thermodynamically suppress the formation of detrimental point defect clusters (e.g., [2Cu_Zn + Sn_Zn]) and concurrently promote a more favorable band alignment and reduced defect density at the CZTSSe/CdS heterointerface, beyond its known benefits for grain growth.
proposals.method: We will fabricate CZTSSe solar cells using a solution-based precursor with Li-addition, followed by a multi-stage selenization process designed to decouple grain nucleation from growth. The key is a prolonged, low-temperature hold (~400°C) to promote precursor homogenization before rapid high-temperature crystallization. Advanced micro-to-atom scale characterizations will be employed: (1) Drive-Level Capacitance Profiling (DLCP) and Admittance Spectroscopy to quantify bulk and interface defect densities, (2) Temperature-dependent current-voltage (J-V-T) analysis to distinguish recombination mechanisms, and (3) High-resolution STEM-EELS and XPS to directly correlate processing conditions with atomic-scale chemistry at the grain interiors and the critical heterointerface.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, causal link between a specific processing parameter (deferred crystallization) and the suppression of specific non-radiative recombination centers, moving beyond empirical grain boundary observations. It will deliver a refined processing roadmap targeting both bulk and interface quality, offering a clear pathway to reduce the Voc deficit in kesterite photovoltaics.
proposals.novelty: While grain growth via liquid-phase assistance is known, systematically isolating its effect on point defect thermodynamics and the buried heterointerface—rather than just grain boundaries—is a novel and crucial distinction. This approach combines a targeted processing strategy with defect-specific diagnostics not commonly applied in tandem to kesterite systems.

proposals.title: Controlling Nucleation Kinetics for Monodisperse CZTS Nanocrystals: A Pathway to Enhanced Kesterite Solar Cell Performance
proposals.motivation: CZTSSe solar cells are promising thin-film photovoltaic absorbers, but their efficiency is limited by bulk and interfacial defects, often linked to non-uniform grain growth and secondary phases. Pre-2026 research, including Green's work on Li-enhanced liquid-phase-assisted grain growth, highlights that superior optoelectronic properties require high-quality, uniform precursor materials. Achieving precise control over nanocrystal size and monodispersity during colloidal synthesis is a critical, unresolved step towards fabricating high-quality CZTSSe absorber layers.
proposals.hypothesis: By deliberately suppressing the initial nucleation burst in hot-injection colloidal synthesis through tailored ligand chemistry and precursor reactivity, we can achieve precise control over CZTS nanocrystal size and monodispersity, which will subsequently lead to more uniform and phase-pure kesterite thin films after selenization.
proposals.method: We will adapt the nucleation suppression concept, established for metal chalcogenides like PbS, to the quaternary CZTS system. A hot-injection synthesis will be performed using metal chloride and thiourea precursors in oleylamine. Nucleation kinetics will be controlled by systematically varying: 1) the injection temperature and precursor concentration to modulate supersaturation, and 2) the use of strongly coordinating ligands (e.g., oleic acid) and pre-formed metal complexes to slow precursor reactivity. The size, distribution, phase purity, and composition of the resulting nanocrystals will be characterized by TEM, XRD, and Raman spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust synthetic protocol for monodisperse CZTS nanocrystals, providing a superior precursor ink for solution-processed photovoltaics. It will directly link nanocrystal uniformity to the microstructure and phase purity of the final absorber film, offering a new materials-centric strategy to reduce defect density and improve carrier collection efficiency in kesterite solar cells, as tracked in the Solar Cell Efficiency Tables.
proposals.novelty: While nucleation control is known for binary systems, its deliberate application to the complex, multi-cation CZTS synthesis to govern monodispersity is unexplored. This approach moves beyond typical post-synthetic size selection and directly addresses the fundamental kinetic bottleneck limiting high-quality nanocrystal production for kesterites.

proposals.title: Engineering Heteroepitaxial Nucleation for High-Efficiency, Ultra-Thin CZTS Solar Cells via Functional Cap Layers
proposals.motivation: Kesterite CZTSSe solar cells require high-quality, large-grain absorber layers for efficient carrier transport, but achieving this in ultra-thin (<500 nm), conformal films is challenging due to limited nucleation density and poor grain growth. This thickness reduction is critical for material savings and flexible photovoltaics, yet efficiency remains well below the Shockley-Queisser limit, largely due to high recombination at grain boundaries in thin films. An inverse-design approach to nucleation control, rather than post-deposition grain growth, is an underexplored pathway to improve film quality from the bottom up.
proposals.hypothesis: That a specifically engineered inorganic cap layer (e.g., ZnS, SnS2, or a tailored oxide) deposited prior to CZTS can act as a heteroepitaxial template, significantly increasing nucleation density and promoting the formation of large, oriented grains during the subsequent sulfurization/selenization of an ultra-thin metallic precursor.
proposals.method: We will deposit ultra-thin (300 nm) Cu-Zn-Sn metallic precursors via sputtering onto Mo-coated glass with and without various nano-scale cap layers. Cap layers (2-10 nm) of ZnS, SnS2, and Al-doped ZnO will be tested. Sulfurization will be performed in a controlled atmosphere furnace. The primary characterization will combine scanning electron microscopy for grain morphology with electron backscatter diffraction for grain orientation. We will employ finite-element modeling (COMSOL) of nucleation kinetics under the strain and interfacial energy conditions imposed by each cap layer to guide layer selection and interpret results. Finished devices will be fabricated and characterized by J-V and EQE measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel materials processing strategy for controlling the initial stages of kesterite film formation, directly addressing the grain boundary problem in ultra-thin absorbers. It will provide a dataset linking specific cap layer properties (lattice mismatch, surface energy) to nucleation density and final photovoltaic performance, offering a new degree of freedom for CZTSSe device engineering beyond traditional doping or post-growth treatments.
proposals.novelty: While previous work (e.g., Li-enhanced liquid-phase grain growth) focuses on manipulating grain growth *after* nucleation, this proposal inverts the problem by deliberately designing a substrate interface to *control the nucleation event itself* for kesterites, a concept not systematically explored pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Sequential Ge-Capping and Selenization Strategy to Mitigate Voltage Deficits in Wide-Bandgap CZTSSe Solar Cells
proposals.motivation: Kesterite CZTSSe solar cells suffer from a large open-circuit voltage (VOC) deficit, which is a primary limitation to their efficiency, as highlighted in the Solar Cell Efficiency Tables. This deficit is particularly severe in wider-bandgap CZTS (Eg ~1.5 eV), which otherwise offers a better theoretical match to the solar spectrum. Recent work, including by this group, has shown that Ge incorporation can passivate defects and enhance grain growth, but its application has been largely confined to low-bandgap CZTSe or CZTSSe.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a Ge-rich surface layer (a 'Ge cap') during the processing of a wide-bandgap CZTS precursor will first improve VOC by suppressing non-radiative recombination at grain boundaries, and that subsequent controlled selenization of this structure will fine-tune the bandgap and improve carrier collection for a higher overall efficiency.
proposals.method: We will fabricate CZTS precursors via solution processing or sputtering to target a bandgap of ~1.5 eV. A thin Ge layer will be thermally evaporated onto the precursor surface prior to high-temperature annealing. The role of the Ge cap in modifying grain growth, bulk, and interface defects will be characterized using techniques like STEM-EELS and DLTS, as employed in our prior work. Subsequently, a controlled selenization step will be performed in a two-zone furnace to create a graded CZTSSe absorber with a Ge-enriched back region, optimizing the band alignment and optical absorption.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel processing pathway to decouple VOC improvement from current (JSC) optimization in kesterite solar cells. It is expected to yield a certified device efficiency exceeding 14% for a wide-bandgap CZTSSe cell, contributing directly to the efficiency tables. The study will deliver fundamental insights into the dual role of Ge as a VOC-enhancing agent and a facilitator for controlled anion alloying.
proposals.novelty: While Ge doping and Se alloying are known individually, the sequential, spatially controlled application of a Ge cap *before* selenization to engineer a graded absorber specifically for high-bandgap CZTS is a novel and non-obvious synthesis strategy not reported in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Dominant Role of Bulk Crystallinity in Limiting CZTSSe Solar Cell Performance: A Comparative Study of Grain Growth and Interface Passivation
proposals.motivation: Despite extensive interface engineering, CZTSSe solar cell efficiencies remain far below the Shockley-Queisser limit and their CIGS counterparts, with a record of only ~13% as of 2025. Recent work, including Dr. Green's own on Li-enhanced grain growth, suggests that bulk defects and small grains may be a more fundamental bottleneck than interface recombination. This proposal directly tests the prevailing assumption that interface quality is the primary limitation.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic enhancement of bulk CZTSSe crystallinity—specifically grain size and intragrain order—will yield greater improvements in open-circuit voltage (Voc) and fill factor (FF) than equivalent effort spent on optimizing the CdS/CZTSSe heterojunction or back-contact interface alone.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a series of CZTSSe absorbers using a co-evaporation process, varying post-deposition annealing parameters (temperature, time, Se/S atmosphere) and incorporating LiF precursor layers to promote liquid-phase-assisted grain growth, as per our recent method. For each bulk condition, we will apply three distinct interface treatments: a standard chemical bath deposited (CBD) CdS buffer, a Zn(O,S) buffer deposited by atomic layer deposition (ALD), and an In2S3 buffer. Device performance will be characterized by J-V and EQE, while bulk vs. interface effects will be decoupled using drive-level capacitance profiling (DLCP) to measure bulk defect density and temperature-dependent J-V analysis to quantify interface recombination velocity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, experimentally grounded hierarchy of loss mechanisms in CZTSSe, clarifying whether the community's focus should shift more decisively towards bulk material synthesis. It will establish clear processing pathways for achieving large-grained, low-defect-density absorbers and define the efficiency gains achievable from bulk improvement alone.
proposals.novelty: While interface studies are abundant, this is a controlled, side-by-side experimental decoupling of bulk and interface contributions, moving beyond correlative observations. It directly challenges the implicit prioritization in much recent literature by using the same advanced characterization tools typically applied to interfaces to rigorously assess the bulk.

proposals.title: Overcoming the 10% Efficiency Ceiling in Heat-Treated CZTS Solar Cells via Defect-Engineered Grain Boundary Passivation
proposals.motivation: Kesterite CZTSSe solar cells have stagnated near 10-13% efficiency, with heat-treated pure-sulfide CZTS devices often reported below 10%, suggesting a fundamental performance ceiling. This perceived limit, attributed to intrinsic defects and recombination at grain boundaries, hinders the development of CZTS as a sustainable, earth-abundant alternative to CIGS and CdTe photovoltaics. Challenging this ceiling is critical to unlocking the full potential of this material system for terawatt-scale solar deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: The 10% efficiency limit in heat-treated CZTS devices is not intrinsic but is imposed by a combination of non-optimal grain boundary chemistry and unpassivated deep defects, which can be overcome through targeted post-deposition treatments and cation substitution.
proposals.method: We will fabricate CZTS absorbers via a two-stage process (sputtering of metallic precursors followed by sulfurization). The core approach involves a systematic study of post-heat-treatment in controlled atmospheres (e.g., Sn/S2 vapor) combined with extrinsic alkali (Li, Na) and cation (Ge, Sb) incorporation, building on recent findings. Advanced micro-to-atom scale characterization (STEM-EELS, APT, DLTS) will be used to map the correlation between treatment conditions, grain boundary composition (e.g., SnOx, Cu-rich phases), point defect populations (V_S, Cu_Zn), and electronic performance parameters (Voc, Jsc, FF) extracted from completed solar cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, mechanism-based assessment of whether the 10% barrier is a practical or fundamental limit for CZTS. It will deliver a processing roadmap for defect and grain boundary engineering specific to heat-treated devices, directly aiming to push certified cell efficiencies beyond 12%. The findings will clarify the role of heat treatment environments in final device performance.
proposals.novelty: While alkali and Ge incorporation are known, their synergistic application specifically to overcome the 10% ceiling in *heat-treated* CZTS (not selenized CZTSSe) via deliberate grain boundary modification is unexplored. The proposal directly links advanced atomic-scale diagnostics with targeted processing to challenge a widely accepted performance plateau.

proposals.title: Systematic Optimization of CdS/CZTS Heterojunction Annealing for Enhanced Efficiency via Defect Passivation and Band Alignment
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, kesterite CZTS solar cells have a certified record efficiency of only ~13%, significantly below the theoretical Shockley-Queisser limit, largely due to high open-circuit voltage (Voc) deficits. The post-deposition heat treatment (PDHT) of the heterojunction is a critical, yet poorly optimized, process step known to influence interface recombination, band alignment, and defect chemistry, directly impacting Voc and fill factor. A systematic study is urgently needed to move beyond the empirical, sub-10% efficiency plateau common in many labs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a precise, multi-variable optimization of the CdS/CZTS heterojunction PDHT—controlling temperature, duration, and atmosphere—will minimize interfacial defect density and optimize conduction band offset, thereby significantly reducing the Voc deficit and pushing device efficiency beyond 10% in a reproducible manner.
proposals.method: We will fabricate baseline CZTS absorber layers via a Li-enhanced liquid-phase-assisted process to ensure large grains. A combinatorial matrix of PDHT parameters will be executed: temperature (180-260°C), time (5-30 min), and atmosphere (N2, N2+O2, N2+H2S). Devices will be completed with standard ZnO/ITO layers. The primary diagnostic will be current density-voltage analysis under AM1.5G illumination. Complementary characterization will include temperature-dependent Voc analysis to quantify recombination activation energy, capacitance-voltage profiling for carrier density, and XPS/UPS for band alignment shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative processing-property relationship for the critical heterojunction annealing step in CZTS photovoltaics. It will provide a clear, optimized recipe to overcome the 10% efficiency barrier and identify the dominant physical mechanism—whether defect passivation or band alignment modification—responsible for the gain. The findings will be a crucial reference point for future interface engineering in kesterite and related thin-film solar cells.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have varied single PDHT parameters, our approach is novel in its systematic, multi-dimensional optimization specifically targeting the heterojunction, directly linking process conditions to fundamental electronic losses (Voc deficit) rather than just final efficiency. It integrates Green's recent insights on Li-enhanced grain growth with precise interface control.

proposals.title: Tailoring Nanoscale Grain Growth and Carrier Dynamics in CZTS Nanowire Solar Cells via Controlled Post-Deposition Annealing
proposals.motivation: CZTS solar cells suffer from severe voltage deficits and recombination losses, primarily due to bulk and interface defects. Nanostructured CZTS (e.g., nanowires, quantum dots) offers a promising route to enhanced light absorption and carrier collection, but its efficiency is critically limited by poor crystallinity and high surface recombination. Optimizing the post-synthesis thermal processing of these nanostructures is a key, unresolved challenge to unlock their potential.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a precisely controlled, multi-stage heat treatment protocol will simultaneously enhance grain growth within CZTS nanowires, passivate surface defects, and improve the nanowire/buffer layer heterojunction, leading to a significant reduction in non-radiative recombination and an increase in open-circuit voltage.
proposals.method: CZTS nanowire arrays will be synthesized via a solution-based method on Mo-coated glass. A systematic annealing study will be conducted using a rapid thermal processing furnace, varying temperature (350-550°C), duration, and atmosphere (Se/S vapor, N2). The role of alkali metals (Li, Na) introduced during synthesis or post-deposition will be investigated to promote liquid-phase-assisted grain growth. Devices will be completed with standard CdS/i-ZnO/ITO layers. Characterization will combine J-V, EQE, and TRPL with advanced micro-to-nanoscale techniques (HR-TEM, EDS, XPS) to correlate processing conditions with nanostructure evolution and electronic properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first detailed processing-structure-property relationship for annealed CZTS nanowire solar cells, directly linking thermal protocols to nanoscale grain growth and device performance. It will provide a concrete pathway to improve the VOC and efficiency of nanostructured kesterite devices, contributing valuable knowledge to the broader field of solution-processed inorganic photovoltaics.
proposals.novelty: While heat treatments for CZTS thin films are well-studied, their application and optimization for defined nanowire geometries is unexplored. The novel integration of alkali-enhanced grain growth concepts, proven in thin films, into a nanowire-specific annealing protocol represents a distinct and non-obvious advancement.

proposals.title: Continuous Rapid Thermal Processing for Roll-to-Roll Fabrication of Flexible Kesterite CZTSSe Solar Cells
proposals.motivation: Kesterite CZTSSe solar cells are promising for flexible photovoltaics due to their earth-abundant, non-toxic constituents and tunable bandgap. However, their commercialization is hindered by batch-processing methods, particularly the critical selenization/sulfurization annealing step, which is incompatible with high-throughput, low-cost roll-to-roll (R2R) manufacturing. Developing a continuous annealing process is essential to bridge the efficiency potential demonstrated in lab-scale cells (as tracked in Solar Cell Efficiency Tables) with the scalable production needed for market impact.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a precisely controlled, continuous rapid thermal processing (RTP) profile can induce liquid-phase-assisted grain growth in CZTSSe precursors on flexible substrates, replicating the beneficial microstructural effects of batch annealing while enabling uniform, high-quality film formation in a moving web.
proposals.method: The project will design and construct a custom R2R-compatible annealing module with multiple independently controlled heating zones under controlled Se/S vapor pressure. Precursor stacks (e.g., sputtered metal layers or nanoparticle inks) on flexible polyimide or steel foil will be processed. We will systematically vary web speed (1-10 m/min), peak temperature profile (500-600°C), and dwell time, informed by the known kinetics of kesterite formation and grain growth. Advanced characterization (in-situ XRD, post-process SEM/EBSD, and device testing) will correlate processing parameters with grain morphology, phase purity (suppressing secondary phases), and final photovoltaic performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first demonstrated protocol for continuous annealing of CZTSSe absorbers, directly addressing the manufacturing bottleneck. It will establish the processing window for achieving device-quality films on moving substrates and provide fundamental insights into the kinetics of grain growth under dynamic thermal conditions. The outcome is a critical step toward viable R2R production of flexible kesterite PV.
proposals.novelty: While batch-process optimization for CZTSSe is well-studied, the translation to a continuous, moving-substrate thermal process with precise atmosphere and temperature control is unexplored. The novelty lies in engineering the dynamic thermal gradient and reaction environment to control phase formation and grain growth in real-time during web transport.

proposals.title: Enhancing CZTS/C-Si Tandem Solar Cell Performance via Heterojunction Heat Treatment of the Wide-Gap CZTS Top Cell
proposals.motivation: CZTSSe solar cells, with a tunable bandgap of ~1.0-1.5 eV, are promising wide-gap top-cell candidates for tandem devices with silicon bottom cells, potentially exceeding the single-junction Shockley-Queisser limit. However, their efficiency is limited by bulk and interface defects, including small grains and unfavorable band alignments. Addressing these limitations in the specific context of a tandem stack is critical for next-generation photovoltaics.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a controlled heterojunction heat treatment (HHT) to the CZTS/CdS interface within a monolithic tandem device will simultaneously enhance grain growth and improve the conduction band offset, leading to reduced recombination and improved carrier collection specifically in the top cell, thereby boosting the overall tandem efficiency.
proposals.method: We will fabricate monolithic two-terminal CZTS/C-Si tandem cells using a sputtered CZTS precursor and a standard n-type c-Si bottom cell with a tunnel junction. The key intervention is a post-deposition HHT (200-300°C in inert/S atmosphere) applied only after the CdS buffer layer deposition on the CZTS absorber. This will be compared to standard selenization. Characterization will include cross-sectional SEM for grain structure, J-V and EQE measurements for sub-cell performance, and Kelvin probe force microscopy to analyze the heterojunction band alignment.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct process-structure-property relationship for HHT in tandem architectures, providing a targeted method to improve the voltage and current of the CZTS sub-cell. It will yield a practical pathway to enhance the efficiency of low-cost, all-thin-film tandem solar cells, contributing valuable data to the 'Solar cell efficiency tables'.
proposals.novelty: While HHT is known for single-junction CZTS cells, its application and effects are unexplored in the constrained thermal budget and complex interfacial environment of a monolithic tandem stack, where it must not degrade the underlying silicon cell or tunnel junction.

proposals.title: A Framework for Defining and Reporting Power Conversion Efficiency in Shape-Conforming and Flexible Kesterite Solar Cells
proposals.motivation: The standard photovoltaic efficiency metric, defined as power output divided by fixed-aperture area, is inadequate for emerging flexible and shape-conforming solar cells. For applications like building-integrated PV (BIPV), wearable electronics, and curved surfaces, the active, light-harvesting area can differ significantly from a rigid, planar reference, leading to misrepresentation of true device performance. This ambiguity hinders fair comparison and slows the development of non-planar thin-film technologies like CZTSSe.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a new efficiency reporting framework, incorporating both a 'conformable active area' and a standardized 'illumination-corrected effective area,' will provide a more accurate and physically meaningful performance metric for flexible kesterite solar cells, enabling better technological benchmarking.
proposals.method: We will fabricate flexible CZTSSe solar cells on polyimide substrates using established solution-based processes and Li-enhanced grain growth techniques. For efficiency assessment, we will develop a two-tier measurement protocol: 1) A 'conformable active area' measured via high-resolution laser scanning of the physically curved cell surface. 2) An 'illumination-corrected effective area' calculated by a ray-tracing algorithm that accounts for oblique light incidence on non-planar geometries under standard AM1.5G spectrum. Efficiency will be reported using both definitions and compared to the traditional fixed-aperture method.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the thin-film PV community with a concrete, implementable methodology for reporting efficiency in non-planar photovoltaics, moving beyond the fixed-area paradigm. It will establish clearer performance benchmarks for flexible CZTSSe and related kesterite cells, directly supporting their development for next-generation applications. The proposed framework could be adopted for future editions of the Solar Cell Efficiency Tables.
proposals.novelty: While efficiency reporting standards are well-defined for rigid cells, applying advanced geometric characterization and illumination modeling to define a cell's 'true' working area for flexible PV is a novel, critical step. This directly addresses a fundamental measurement gap that has been overlooked in the pursuit of higher grain quality and band-gap engineering in kesterites.

proposals.title: A Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) Verification Protocol for Kesterite and Related Thin-Film Photovoltaics
proposals.motivation: The field of emerging thin-film photovoltaics, including kesterites (CZTSSe), perovskites, and organics, is currently benchmarked almost exclusively by peak laboratory efficiency, as reflected in seminal publications like the 'Solar cell efficiency tables.' This creates a misalignment between academic research priorities and the commercial imperatives of low-cost, durable energy generation. A verification framework that directly quantifies the economic drivers of LCOE is urgently needed to guide material and process innovations toward market viability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a standardized, LCOE-centric verification protocol, incorporating accelerated stability testing and realistic performance factors, will reveal a different ranking of emerging PV technologies and identify specific, non-obvious material and process improvements (e.g., grain growth enhancers, defect passivation) that maximize energy yield per dollar rather than peak efficiency.
proposals.method: The protocol will be developed by first defining a simplified LCOE model with inputs of stabilized efficiency (from light-soaking and thermal cycling), degradation rate (from ISOS-L-2 accelerated tests), spectral and temperature response coefficients, and an estimated material/processing cost per square meter. We will apply this protocol to a set of CZTSSe devices fabricated with variations informed by recent work on Ge incorporation and Li-enhanced grain growth. Critical parameters will be measured under controlled, reproducible conditions mimicking one sun, AM1.5G spectrum at varying temperatures (25-65°C) to generate a normalized 'LCOE score' for each variant.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the PV research community with a practical, transparent tool for evaluating technology progress toward economic competitiveness. It will directly link fundamental material science, such as the role of grain boundaries and band alignment, to financial performance metrics, offering investors and manufacturers a clearer benchmark beyond efficiency records. For kesterite research, it will quantitatively demonstrate the LCOE impact of strategies like liquid-phase-assisted grain growth.
proposals.novelty: While efficiency tables and stability tests exist separately, the novelty lies in their integration into a single, cost-weighted verification standard designed specifically for emerging thin films, shifting the benchmark from a singular material property (peak η) to a system-level economic output (¢/kWh).

proposals.title: A Standardized Protocol for Independent Efficiency Verification of Emerging Kesterite and Chalcogenide Thin-Film Solar Cells
proposals.motivation: The rapid progress in non-silicon thin-film photovoltaics, particularly kesterites (CZTSSe) and related chalcogenides, has led to frequent reports of record efficiencies. However, the lack of a standardized, independent verification process tailored to these materials creates uncertainty in performance benchmarking. This hinders credible comparison, slows technology transfer, and undermines investor confidence, as highlighted by the reliance on self-reported data in many studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by developing and applying a material-specific verification protocol—accounting for unique metastabilities, spectral responses, and measurement artifacts—we can establish reliable, reproducible efficiency benchmarks for emerging thin-film cells, reducing the discrepancy between champion cell reports and independently verified values.
proposals.method: The method will adapt and extend the rigorous procedures used for silicon and established thin-films (like CIGS) to kesterites. This involves: 1) Defining preconditioning light-soaking and bias steps to stabilize known metastable states in CZTSSe. 2) Implementing differential spectral responsivity analysis to accurately deconvolve sub-bandgap absorption and tail states. 3) Utilizing temperature-controlled current-voltage (I-V) and external quantum efficiency (EQE) measurements with calibrated reference cells traceable to national laboratories. 4) Cross-validating results between two independent, accredited labs (e.g., NREL and a partner institute) on a set of identical champion devices.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the photovoltaic community with the first consensus-based framework for independent verification of kesterite and related emerging thin-film solar cells. It will yield a critical, trusted dataset of verified performance metrics, directly informing future entries in the 'Solar Cell Efficiency Tables'. This will accelerate R&D by providing a clear, reliable target for material and process optimization.
proposals.novelty: While verification protocols exist for mature PV technologies, creating a tailored, standardized process that addresses the specific metastabilities and diagnostic challenges of solution-processed, multinary chalcogenides like kesterites is a novel and essential step for the field's maturation.

proposals.title: A Glicko-2-Informed High-Throughput Framework for Accelerated Discovery of Optimal Tandem Solar Cell Architectures
proposals.motivation: The combinatorial space for tandem solar cell materials (e.g., perovskite top cell composition, silicon or CZTS bottom cell, interlayer design) is vast, making exhaustive experimental exploration impractical. Pre-2026, high-throughput methods exist but often rely on static rankings or brute-force screening, lacking a dynamic, uncertainty-aware strategy to prioritize experiments. This inefficiency slows the convergence towards commercially viable, high-efficiency, and stable tandem configurations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that treating tandem cell architectures as competitors in a dynamic league, with their performance (efficiency, stability) modeled by an adapted Glicko-2 rating system, will create a self-optimizing research pipeline that converges on champion designs faster than traditional sequential or purely ranking-based experimental approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a closed-loop computational-experimental framework. A database of candidate tandem designs (varying absorber materials, bandgaps, process conditions) will be initialized with theoretical ratings. After each high-throughput fabrication and characterization cycle (measuring PCE, J-V, stability metrics), results will update each design's Glicko-2 rating, skill score, and rating deviation (uncertainty). The algorithm will then recommend the next batch of experiments by probabilistically simulating 'matches' between candidates, prioritizing high-skill, low-uncertainty designs for validation and high-uncertainty 'dark horses' with strong theoretical potential for exploration.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, adaptive algorithm for navigating complex materials spaces, directly applicable to tandem PV development. It will provide a publicly accessible benchmark dataset of dynamically ranked tandem architectures and demonstrate accelerated experimental convergence to high-performance designs, validated by fabricating champion cells.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the adaptation of the Glicko-2 system—a dynamic Bayesian rating algorithm from competitive gaming—to materials science, enabling quantitative management of uncertainty and strategic exploration-exploitation trade-offs in experimental planning, beyond static efficiency tables or simple ranking.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Production-Average Efficiency Gap in Kesterite and Related Thin-Film Photovoltaics
proposals.motivation: The photovoltaic field, as chronicled in the 'Solar cell efficiency tables', is driven by reports of champion cell efficiencies, which can obscure the performance of commercially relevant production. For emerging absorbers like CZTSSe, the gap between lab-champion cells and average production cells is suspected to be large, hindering accurate assessment of technological readiness and economic potential. A systematic study of median production efficiency is critical for identifying the dominant, yield-limiting defects introduced during scale-up.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the statistical distribution of efficiencies from a production line, centered on the median, will reveal a stronger correlation with specific, process-induced bulk and interfacial defects than champion cell data, providing a clearer roadmap for targeted process improvement.
proposals.method: We will collaborate with industrial partners to obtain large, statistically significant batches (N>1000) of commercially produced CZTSSe solar cells. For each cell, current-voltage and quantum efficiency measurements will be performed under standard test conditions. The data will be analyzed to determine the median, mean, and full distribution of efficiency, fill factor, and open-circuit voltage. Advanced micro-to-atom scale characterizations (EDS, TEM, PL) will be performed on cells selected from across the efficiency distribution to correlate specific defects (e.g., secondary phases, grain boundaries, interfacial recombination) with performance metrics. The analysis will adapt statistical process control methods to photovoltaic manufacturing data.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, industry-relevant benchmark for thin-film PV technologies, moving beyond champion cell reports. It will directly identify the most common efficiency-limiting factors in production-scale kesterite cells, guiding process optimization towards higher yield and average performance. The methodology will provide a template for assessing the production-readiness of other emerging photovoltaic materials.
proposals.novelty: While champion efficiencies are routinely tracked, a rigorous, defect-correlated analysis of median production efficiency for kesterite photovoltaics has not been published. This inverts the traditional research focus from exceptional performance to typical performance, linking statistical process control with fundamental materials characterization.

proposals.title: Scalable Deposition of Ge-Alloyed CZTS Nanoparticle Precursors via a Hybrid Inkjet-Sputtering Process for Large-Area Solar Cells
proposals.motivation: Kesterite CZTSSe solar cells, especially with Ge alloying, show promise for efficient, earth-abundant photovoltaics, but lab-scale efficiencies have not translated to manufacturable, large-area modules. Current nanoparticle ink processes struggle with uniformity and defect control over large substrates, hindering commercialization. A scalable, high-throughput deposition method that preserves the beneficial microstructural and electronic properties of Ge:CZTS is a critical unmet need.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid deposition process, combining inkjet-printed Ge:CZTS nanoparticle layers with a subsequent reactive sputtering step, will enable uniform, large-area absorber films with controlled composition and enhanced grain growth, leading to improved photovoltaic performance on substrate sizes >5x5 cm².
proposals.method: The project will first synthesize Ge-alloyed CZTS nanoparticles via a hot-injection method, optimizing Ge/(Ge+Sn) ratio for ink stability. A custom inkjet printing system will be developed to deposit uniform nanoparticle layers on Mo-coated 10x10 cm² glass substrates, with process parameters (drop spacing, platen temperature) mapped for thickness control. Subsequently, a reactive sputtering step using a Cu-Zn-Sn-S target in a Se-containing atmosphere will be employed to densify the nanoparticle layer, fill voids, and promote large grain growth via a liquid-phase assisted mechanism, as inspired by recent Li-enhanced work. The process will be characterized by XRD, Raman, SEM-EDS, and completed solar cell devices will be tested for J-V and EQE performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a prototype manufacturing process for large-area Ge:CZTSSe absorbers, directly addressing the scale-up bottleneck. It will establish the relationship between hybrid process parameters, film microstructure, and device efficiency, providing a pathway beyond lab-scale spin-coating. The results will be a significant step toward low-cost, high-throughput production of kesterite PV modules.
proposals.novelty: While nanoparticle inks and sputtering exist separately, their sequential integration for kesterite scale-up is unexplored. The novelty lies in using sputtering not for the entire film, but as a post-treatment to transform and perfect a nanoparticle pre-layer, uniquely combining the compositional control of solution processing with the densification and uniformity of vacuum techniques.

proposals.title: Liquid-Phase-Assisted Grain Growth for Enhanced CIGS Solar Cells: A Pathway to High-Efficiency Solution-Processed Absorbers
proposals.motivation: Solution-processed Cu(In,Ga)Se2 (CIGS) solar cells offer a promising low-cost alternative to vacuum-deposited champion devices, but their performance is limited by poor grain growth and high defect densities in the absorber layer. Recent work on kesterite CZTSSe has demonstrated that liquid-phase-assisted grain growth (LGG), often enhanced by alkali metals like Li, can dramatically improve grain size and carrier collection. Applying this proven concept to CIGS could bridge the efficiency gap between solution-based and vacuum-based fabrication, a key pre-2026 challenge for scalable thin-film photovoltaics.
proposals.hypothesis: Introducing a controlled, low-melting-point secondary phase during the selenization of solution-deposited CIGS precursor films will induce liquid-phase-assisted grain growth, leading to larger grains, reduced grain boundary recombination, and ultimately higher device efficiency.
proposals.method: We will fabricate CIGS solar cells from aqueous/ethanol-based precursor solutions containing Cu, In, and Ga salts. The LGG process will be introduced by incorporating controlled amounts of Li salts (e.g., LiF) and selenium into the precursor, or by depositing a Li-Se containing layer on top. Annealing will be performed in a sealed tube furnace with a controlled Se atmosphere. The temperature ramp profile will be optimized to first form a liquid Li-Se or related phase that facilitates atomic mobility and grain coalescence, followed by a higher-temperature step to ensure full crystallization and composition homogenization. Advanced characterization (SEM, XRD, EBIC) will correlate processing with grain structure and electronic properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel processing route for high-quality solution-processed CIGS absorbers, directly targeting the grain growth bottleneck. It will provide fundamental insights into the role of liquid phases in chalcogenide film crystallization and demonstrate a tangible efficiency improvement for non-vacuum CIGS devices, contributing to the broader goal of low-cost, high-performance photovoltaics.
proposals.novelty: While LGG is established for kesterites, its deliberate application to solution-processed CIGS is novel. The adaptation requires careful tuning of chemistry and annealing to accommodate the different phase equilibria of the Cu-In-Ga-Se system, moving beyond simple alkali post-deposition treatment to active liquid-phase sintering during growth.

proposals.title: Eliminating Secondary Phase Residues at the Back Contact of CZTSSe Solar Cells via Controlled Liquid-Phase-Assisted Grain Growth
proposals.motivation: Kesterite CZTSSe solar cells are limited by low open-circuit voltages, often attributed to recombination at the rear interface. Pre-2026 work, including Green's recent research, shows that liquid-phase-assisted grain growth using additives like Li or Ge is crucial for achieving large grains and high efficiency. However, incomplete consumption or evaporation of this liquid phase can leave detrimental secondary phases (e.g., SnS_x, Li_2S) at the Mo back contact, creating a barrier for hole extraction and enhancing recombination.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by precisely engineering the thermal profile and ambient atmosphere during the selenization/sulfurization process, the liquid phase can be fully consumed in grain growth or evaporated, resulting in a pure, secondary-phase-free CZTSSe/MoSe_2 back interface and improved device performance.
proposals.method: We will fabricate CZTSSe absorbers using a standard solution process with Li and Ge incorporation. The key innovation is a multi-stage rapid thermal processing (RTP) protocol with real-time in-situ mass spectrometry to monitor the evolution of volatile species (SnS, SeS). We will systematically vary the maximum temperature (520-580°C), ramp rates, and dwell times in a controlled S/Se/Ar atmosphere. The resulting films and interfaces will be characterized by high-resolution TEM, EDS, and Raman spectroscopy to map phase purity, complemented by DLTS and J-V analysis to correlate interface quality with device parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct process-structure-property relationship for back interface engineering in CZTSSe, providing a clear pathway to minimize voltage losses. It will deliver a reproducible fabrication protocol for a cleaner back interface, contributing to the goal of pushing CZTSSe efficiencies beyond the current record of ~13%. The findings will be broadly applicable to other chalcogenide thin-film solar cells employing liquid-phase sintering.
proposals.novelty: While liquid-phase-assisted growth is known, prior work focuses on promoting grain growth. This proposal uniquely targets the active removal of the liquid phase *residue* itself as a critical step, using a diagnostic-driven, closed-loop thermal process design informed by in-situ diagnostics, which is not standard in the field.

proposals.title: Low-Temperature, Solution-Processed CZTSSe Solar Cells via Liquid-Phase-Assisted Nanocrystal Fusion and Cation Exchange
proposals.motivation: High-temperature annealing (>500°C) for kesterite CZTSSe grain growth induces detrimental secondary phases, Sn loss, and voids, limiting efficiency. It also precludes low-cost, flexible substrates. Pre-2026 work, including Green's own on Li-enhanced liquid-phase-assisted growth, suggests alternative pathways exist but still rely on significant thermal input.
proposals.hypothesis: That a sequential, low-temperature (<250°C) process combining colloidal nanocrystal sintering with a reactive liquid-phase chalcogenide flux can drive direct nanocrystal-to-grain transformation and precise cation exchange (e.g., Zn/Sn ordering) without conventional high-temperature crystallization.
proposals.method: We will synthesize monodisperse Cu2ZnSnS4 nanocrystals with controlled stoichiometry. A thin film will be deposited and treated with a low-melting-point chalcogenide flux (e.g., Na2Se/S or Li2Se/S dissolved in hydrazine/amine mixtures) under mild heating. This liquid phase will facilitate nanocrystal fusion and simultaneous Se incorporation via anion exchange. A final cation-specific chemical bath (e.g., for Zn enrichment) will be applied to fine-tune the near-surface composition and band alignment. Advanced characterization (TEM, Raman, GIXRD) will monitor phase evolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for sub-250°C fabrication of phase-pure, large-grained CZTSSe, directly challenging the field's thermal paradigm. It will provide a fundamental understanding of liquid-phase-assisted solid-state reactions in nanocrystal systems and open a route to efficient kesterite cells on polymer substrates.
proposals.novelty: While liquid-phase-assisted growth is known, its use for complete low-temperature synthesis from nanocrystals, decoupling grain growth from high-temperature selenization, is unexplored. The approach radically departs from annealing-based crystallization, targeting the kinetic, not thermodynamic, control of grain and phase formation.

proposals.title: High-Efficiency Flexible Pure-Sulfide Cu2ZnSnS4 Solar Cells via Lithium-Assisted Low-Temperature Grain Growth
proposals.motivation: Kesterite solar cells, particularly CZTSSe, rely on complex, high-temperature selenization processes that are incompatible with flexible polymer substrates and introduce manufacturing complexity. As of 2025, the record efficiency for pure-sulfide CZTS lags significantly behind its selenized counterpart, largely due to poor grain growth and high defect density. Developing a high-performance pure-sulfide process is critical for enabling low-cost, lightweight, flexible photovoltaics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strategic incorporation of lithium during a low-temperature, liquid-phase-assisted annealing process can dramatically enhance grain growth and passivate defects in pure-sulfide CZTS absorbers, enabling flexible solar cells with efficiencies exceeding 10% without the need for selenization.
proposals.method: We will fabricate CZTS precursors via non-vacuum nanoparticle ink deposition on flexible polyimide substrates. The key innovation is a two-stage annealing process: a low-temperature (<450°C) treatment in a sulfur atmosphere with controlled LiF incorporation, designed to promote a transient liquid phase for grain boundary migration, followed by a rapid thermal processing step. Advanced characterization (including in-situ XRD, Raman, and TEM) will correlate Li distribution with grain morphology and electronic properties. Device optimization will focus on band alignment engineering at the CdS/CZTS interface.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, selenization-free fabrication route for kesterite photovoltaics directly on flexible substrates. It will provide a fundamental understanding of lithium's role in sulfide grain growth and defect passivation. The project aims to set a new efficiency benchmark for flexible pure-sulfide CZTS cells, directly addressing a key bottleneck noted in the Solar Cell Efficiency Tables.
proposals.novelty: While Li-enhanced grain growth has been explored for CZTSSe, its application to enable high-quality, large-grained pure-sulfide CZTS at substrate-compatible low temperatures is novel. The approach challenges the prevailing assumption that selenization or high-temperature processing (>500°C) is indispensable for high-performance kesterite devices.

proposals.title: High-Temperature-Stable Polyimide Substrates with Inorganic Diffusion Barriers for Enhanced CZTSSe Grain Growth on Flexible Platforms
proposals.motivation: Flexible kesterite (CZTSSe) photovoltaics promise applications in building-integrated and portable power, but their efficiency lags behind rigid counterparts, primarily due to inferior absorber quality. This stems from the need for low-temperature processing (<450°C) on conventional flexible substrates (e.g., polyimide), which limits grain growth and defect passivation. Enabling higher-temperature annealing (>550°C) on flexible backplanes is a critical, unresolved materials challenge to unlock the full potential of CZTSSe.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering a polyimide substrate with an integrated, thin, and adherent inorganic barrier layer (e.g., Al2O3 or SiO2) will significantly enhance its thermal stability and suppress detrimental diffusion of substrate elements, thereby permitting annealing temperatures sufficient for liquid-phase-assisted grain growth and improved CZTSSe absorber quality.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize novel polyimide precursor formulations, incorporating inorganic nanofillers (e.g., silica nanoparticles) to enhance intrinsic thermal stability. A key step is the atomic layer deposition (ALD) of dense, sub-100 nm Al2O3 or SiO2 barrier layers directly onto cured polyimide. Standard CZTSSe absorber layers will be deposited via solution processing on these engineered substrates. The thermal stability will be quantified using thermogravimetric analysis and in-situ stress measurements during thermal cycling. The primary metric is solar cell performance (J-V, EQE) after a two-stage selenization/sulfurization anneal at target temperatures of 550°C and 580°C, correlated with grain structure (SEM), composition (EDS), and defect density (DLCP) analyses.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a materials platform for high-temperature processing on flexible substrates, directly addressing a major bottleneck in CZTSSe technology. It is expected to yield a record efficiency for flexible CZTSSe devices by enabling the beneficial grain growth and defect passivation mechanisms observed in rigid glass-based cells. The findings will provide a generalizable substrate engineering strategy applicable to other chalcogenide thin-film photovoltaics.
proposals.novelty: While barrier layers are known, their integration into a holistic flexible substrate system designed specifically for the reactive, high-temperature environment of CZTSSe selenization is unexplored. The novelty lies in co-optimizing the polymer matrix and the inorganic barrier as a single, compliant stack to achieve unprecedented thermal stability for chalcogenide processing.

proposals.title: A Flexible Substrate Platform for High-Efficiency, Low-Temperature Deposition of Emerging Thin-Film Photovoltaics
proposals.motivation: Flexible, lightweight photovoltaics are critical for applications like building-integrated PV, portable electronics, and space power. While CZTSSe research has pioneered low-temperature, non-vacuum processing on flexible polymer substrates like polyimide, this platform has not been systematically applied to other promising, non-sulfide/kesterite absorbers. Demonstrating a universal flexible substrate process could accelerate the development of multiple emerging PV technologies beyond rigid glass.
proposals.hypothesis: The optimized substrate stack, thermal budget, and grain-growth promoters (e.g., Li-enhanced liquid-phase processing) developed for high-efficiency flexible CZTSSe cells can be successfully transferred to other emerging thin-film absorbers, such as Sb2Se3 or AgBiS2, to achieve superior grain structure and device performance on flexible substrates compared to conventional approaches.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a baseline flexible substrate stack (polyimide/Mo/Na-containing precursor) optimized for CZTSSe. Using this identical platform, we will deposit Sb2Se3 and AgBiS2 absorber layers via low-temperature (<450°C) close-space sublimation and solution processing, respectively. Advanced micro-to-atom scale characterizations (STEM-EELS, APT) will correlate substrate-induced microstructure (grain size, orientation, defects) with device metrics. The role of Li-doping or other grain-growth enhancers from the substrate will be investigated through intentional diffusion studies and DLCP analysis of carrier collection.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a validated, generic substrate recipe for high-performance flexible thin-film PV, moving beyond material-specific optimization. It will provide fundamental insights into how substrate properties control nucleation and grain growth in diverse absorber systems. The project will deliver prototype flexible solar cells with new absorber materials, reporting their efficiency in the context of the latest Solar Cell Efficiency Tables.
proposals.novelty: The inverse approach—using a substrate platform refined for one material class (kesterites) as a universal testbed for others—is novel. It systematically investigates substrate-driven grain growth across chemically distinct absorbers, a concept not explored in the existing literature focused on absorber-first development.

proposals.title: Structural Photovoltaics: Monolithic Integration of Flexible CZTSSe Solar Cells as Dual-Purpose Power and Load-Bearing Elements
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of portable and wearable electronics demands power sources that are not only lightweight and efficient but also conformable and integrable. Flexible thin-film solar cells, particularly kesterite CZTSSe, offer a promising solution due to their tunable band gap and non-toxic, abundant constituents. However, they are typically treated as add-on power generators, missing the opportunity to serve as multifunctional structural components that simultaneously bear mechanical loads and supply power, thereby enhancing device compactness and energy density.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by engineering the grain structure and interfacial layers of flexible CZTSSe cells, we can create photovoltaic devices that maintain high power conversion efficiency under repeated mechanical strain while providing sufficient structural integrity to act as a primary load-bearing element in small-scale devices.
proposals.method: We will fabricate flexible CZTSSe solar cells on polyimide substrates using a Li-enhanced liquid-phase-assisted grain growth process to achieve large, defect-tolerant grains for improved carrier collection and mechanical robustness. The cells will be monolithically integrated into a prototype device frame (e.g., a wearable sensor housing). We will employ finite element analysis (FEA) to model stress distribution and iteratively design cell geometry and encapsulation. Performance will be evaluated by concurrently measuring photovoltaic parameters (J-V, EQE) and mechanical properties (tensile strength, fatigue resistance under cyclic bending) using a custom in-situ characterization setup.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational framework for 'structural photovoltaics' using earth-abundant materials, providing quantitative metrics for the trade-off between photovoltaic efficiency and mechanical load-bearing capacity. It will deliver a prototype demonstrating the dual functionality, pushing CZTSSe technology beyond conventional applications towards truly integrated energy solutions.
proposals.novelty: While flexible CZTSSe cell efficiency has been studied, their deliberate design and validation as primary structural elements is unexplored. This approach moves beyond mere flexibility to functional mechanical integration, a significant departure from existing research focused solely on electrical performance.

proposals.title: A Framework for Standardized Efficiency Reporting in Nanostructured Photochemical Solar Cells: Application to Quantum Dot and Dye-Sensitized Architectures
proposals.motivation: The rapid progress in emerging photovoltaics like quantum dot (QD) and dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) is hindered by inconsistent measurement and reporting practices. As highlighted in the 'Solar cell efficiency tables', standardized protocols for established technologies like silicon and CZTS have been crucial for credible progress. The lack of such guidelines for nanostructured, often non-equilibrium, photochemical cells leads to inflated efficiency claims and unreliable comparisons, stalling industrial adoption and fundamental understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that developing and applying a specific set of measurement guidelines, accounting for the unique charge dynamics and spectral sensitivity of QD and DSSC devices, will significantly reduce reported performance variance and establish a more reliable benchmark for true technological progress in these fields.
proposals.method: The method will adapt and extend the rigorous protocols used for kesterite (CZTS) cells, as informed by our group's expertise in advanced characterization. We will first conduct a meta-analysis of high-impact literature (pre-2026) to quantify reporting inconsistencies. A core experimental task will involve fabricating reference QD and DSSC devices and subjecting them to controlled variable testing (light bias, spectral mismatch, scan rate, stabilization time). Specific algorithms for correcting spectral mismatch factors for typical QD/DSSC absorption profiles and for quantifying hysteresis in current-voltage curves will be developed. The final output will be a proposed checklist and data reporting template.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a concrete, experimentally-vetted proposal for standardized efficiency measurement and reporting for QD and DSSC technologies. It will directly contribute to more credible entries in future 'Solar cell efficiency tables' for these categories. By reducing measurement artifact, it will accelerate meaningful materials optimization, akin to the role standardization played for CZTSSe.
proposals.novelty: While standardization exists for bulk and thin-film photovoltaics, a targeted framework addressing the specific instability, spectral, and capacitive characteristics of solution-processed nanostructured and photochemical cells is novel. The approach uniquely leverages deep characterization experience from the kesterite field to solve a parallel problem in adjacent emerging PV technologies.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Impact of Realistic Grain Boundary and Impurity Distributions on CIGSe Solar Cell Performance via Coupled Kinetic Monte Carlo and Drift-Diffusion Simulation
proposals.motivation: CIGSe solar cell efficiency improvements have plateaued, with champion devices still far from their theoretical limits. While simulation models often assume ideal, single-crystal-like absorber layers, real CIGSe films contain complex grain boundary (GB) networks and unintentional impurities (e.g., Na, K from soda-lime glass). The precise role of these ubiquitous, non-ideal features in limiting voltage and carrier collection remains poorly quantified, hindering targeted process improvements.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatial correlation of specific GB electronic properties (band bending, recombination velocity) with impurity segregation profiles creates percolative pathways for non-radiative recombination and carrier trapping, which is a primary factor limiting open-circuit voltage (Voc) in state-of-the-art CIGSe devices.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-scale simulation framework. First, a kinetic Monte Carlo (kMC) model, parameterized with pre-2026 experimental data (e.g., from atom probe tomography), will generate realistic 3D polycrystalline CIGSe morphologies with statistically accurate GB networks and impurity distributions. This structural output will inform a 3D finite-element drift-diffusion solver (e.g., Sentaurus TCAD or a custom Poisson-drift-diffusion solver) where GBs are explicitly meshed with locally modified defect densities and band alignments derived from the kMC output. The simulation will be validated against the current-voltage and quantum efficiency data of well-characterized reference cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, microstructure-aware model linking specific fabrication conditions (e.g., substrate type, post-deposition treatments) to predicted device performance. It will identify which GB/impurity characteristics are most detrimental, offering a roadmap for passivation strategies. The open-source simulation framework will be a valuable tool for the thin-film photovoltaic community.
proposals.novelty: While GBs in CIGSe have been studied, simulations typically treat them as uniform, isolated entities. The novelty lies in explicitly modeling the *statistical ensemble* of GBs with correlated impurity decoration, moving beyond the ideal single-boundary assumption to capture the collective impact of a realistic polycrystalline microstructure on device metrics.

proposals.title: Bandgap Engineering of ZrS2 via Alloying for Optimized Band Alignment in CZTSSe Solar Cells
proposals.motivation: Kesterite CZTSSe solar cells suffer from significant open-circuit voltage deficits, partly attributed to unfavorable band alignment at the buffer/absorber interface. ZrS2 has emerged as a promising Cd-free buffer layer, but its fixed bandgap (~1.7 eV) limits the tunability of its conduction band minimum for optimal alignment with CZTSSe. This project addresses a critical materials bottleneck by exploring the electronic property tuning of ZrS2 to enhance device efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that alloying ZrS2 with isovalent (e.g., Hf, Ti) or aliovalent (e.g., Se, O) elements will systematically tune its bandgap and band positions, enabling a near-ideal 'spike-like' conduction band alignment with CZTSSe absorbers and thereby reducing interface recombination.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) calculations using the VASP code with the HSE06 hybrid functional to screen candidate alloys (e.g., Zr_xHf_{1-x}S2, ZrS_{2(1-y)}Se_{2y}) for their electronic structure. Promising compositions will be synthesized via atomic layer deposition (ALD) to ensure conformal, thin-film growth. The films will be integrated into glass/Mo/CZTSSe/ZrS2-alloy/ZnO/ITO device stacks, with performance characterized by J-V, EQE, and temperature-dependent VOC measurements to quantify alignment improvements.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational materials library of tunable ZrS2-based buffer layers, directly linking atomic-scale alloying to device performance. It will provide a new strategy to mitigate voltage losses in kesterite photovoltaics, contributing to the broader goal of developing efficient, earth-abundant thin-film solar cells.
proposals.novelty: While bandgap engineering of absorbers is common, the deliberate, DFT-guided tuning of a wide-bandgap sulfide buffer layer's electronic properties for specific alignment with kesterites is a novel and underexplored pathway to efficiency gains, moving beyond the paradigm of fixed buffer materials.

proposals.title: Experimental Realization and Validation of a High-Efficiency ZrS2/CIGSe Heterojunction Solar Cell
proposals.motivation: CIGSe solar cells have reached certified efficiencies above 23%, but further gains are limited by recombination at the CdS buffer layer interface. Pre-2026 computational studies, including our own simulations, have identified ZrS2 as a promising CdS alternative due to its optimal band alignment, high electron mobility, and potential for reduced interface recombination. Physically fabricating this simulated design is critical to translate theoretical promise into a tangible, high-performance photovoltaic technology.
proposals.hypothesis: That a physically fabricated ZrS2/CIGSe heterojunction solar cell will achieve a power conversion efficiency exceeding 22% by mitigating interface recombination losses through the superior electronic properties of the ZrS2 buffer layer, thereby validating prior simulation results.
proposals.method: We will fabricate the cell using a sequential process: deposition of a standard Mo-coated soda-lime glass substrate, co-evaporation of the CIGSe absorber layer, and atomic layer deposition (ALD) of the ZrS2 buffer layer. This will be followed by RF sputtering of the i-ZnO/ZnO:Al window layer and thermal evaporation of Ni/Al grid contacts. Key to the process is optimizing the ALD parameters for ZrS2 (precursor pulse times, temperature) and implementing a post-deposition low-temperature anneal in Se atmosphere to control the CIGSe/ZrS2 interface quality. Device performance will be characterized by current-voltage measurements under AM1.5G illumination and external quantum efficiency analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental proof-of-concept for a ZrS2-buffered CIGSe solar cell, directly validating a promising computational design. It will yield critical insights into the practical fabrication challenges and interface physics of this novel heterojunction, establishing a new materials pathway for pushing CIGSe efficiencies beyond the CdS limit.
proposals.novelty: While ZrS2 has been studied theoretically and for other applications, its experimental integration as a buffer layer in a complete, high-efficiency CIGSe solar cell device is novel. The proposal uniquely bridges the gap between simulation and fabrication for this specific material system.

proposals.title: First-Principles Screening and Experimental Validation of Alternative Sulfide Buffer Layers for High-Efficiency CIGSe Solar Cells
proposals.motivation: The standard CdS buffer layer in CIGSe photovoltaics presents environmental and performance limitations, including parasitic absorption and a non-ideal conduction band offset. While ZrS2 has emerged as a promising alternative, its long-term stability and scalability remain unproven. A systematic search for other non-toxic, wide-bandgap sulfide buffers is crucial to unlock further efficiency gains and improve process compatibility for CIGSe technology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific metal sulfides (e.g., ZnS, In2S3, SnS2) with tailored electronic structures can form superior heterojunctions with CIGSe, offering improved band alignment, reduced interface recombination, and enhanced stability compared to both CdS and ZrS2.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) simulations using the VASP code to calculate band structures, band offsets, and defect formation energies at the interface between CIGSe and candidate sulfides (ZnS, In2S3, SnS2, SrS). Promising candidates will be fabricated via chemical bath deposition and atomic layer deposition. Devices will be characterized using current-voltage, quantum efficiency, and capacitance-voltage measurements, complemented by XPS and TEM to correlate electronic performance with interfacial chemistry and microstructure.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a materials database of viable sulfide buffers for CIGSe, providing fundamental insights into the role of cation choice on interface electronic properties. It will deliver a validated, high-performance buffer layer candidate that avoids cadmium and potentially outperforms the emerging ZrS2 standard, directly contributing to higher-efficiency, more sustainable thin-film solar cells.
proposals.novelty: While ZrS2 is known, this proposal systematically screens a broader class of sulfide materials using a combined ab-initio and experimental approach specifically targeting improved band alignment and interface passivation, moving beyond simple CdS replacement to engineered buffer design.

proposals.title: In-situ Spectroscopic Ellipsometry for Real-Time Control of ZrS2/CIGSe Heterointerface Formation in Thin-Film Solar Cells
proposals.motivation: The performance of advanced thin-film solar cells, such as those based on CIGSe and emerging kesterite (CZTSSe) absorbers, is critically dependent on the quality and electronic properties of heterointerfaces and buffer layers. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Green's work on grain growth and carrier collection, highlights that uncontrolled layer growth leads to defects and non-ideal band alignments, limiting efficiency. ZrS2 has emerged as a promising, non-toxic alternative buffer layer to CdS, but its integration with CIGSe lacks precise, real-time process control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in-situ spectroscopic ellipsometry (SE) can provide real-time, precise monitoring of the optical constants, thickness, and roughness of both ZrS2 and CIGSe layers during deposition, enabling the controlled formation of an optimal heterointerface with improved band alignment and reduced interface recombination.
proposals.method: We will integrate a spectroscopic ellipsometer (e.g., J.A. Woollam M-2000DI) directly into a multi-stage deposition chamber for sequential growth of CIGSe (via co-evaporation) and ZrS2 (via atomic layer deposition). Real-time ellipsometry data (Ψ, Δ) will be acquired across 0.7–6.5 eV.
