We will employ a multi-layer optical model in the WVASE software, fitting the data using the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm to extract thickness, composition (via dielectric function), and surface roughness with sub-nanometer precision. This feedback will be used to dynamically adjust deposition parameters (temperature, flux rates) for each layer.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel methodology for the real-time, non-destructive characterization and control of complex chalcogenide layer growth. It will provide unprecedented insight into the evolution of the ZrS2/CIGSe interface, directly linking process conditions to optoelectronic properties. The outcome will be a set of optimized, reproducible deposition protocols for high-efficiency, Cd-free thin-film solar cells.
proposals.novelty: While SE is used ex-situ for thin-film analysis, its application for *in-situ*, real-time control of the nascent ZrS2/CIGSe interface—a critical junction for next-generation photovoltaics—is novel. This approach moves beyond post-growth characterization to active process engineering, a significant advance over pre-2026 methods.

proposals.title: Atomic Layer Deposition of ZrS2 as a Tunable Buffer Layer for High-Efficiency CZTSSe Solar Cells
proposals.motivation: Kesterite CZTSSe solar cells suffer from significant open-circuit voltage deficits, partly due to non-ideal band alignment and interface recombination at the buffer/absorber junction. Conventional CdS buffers have a parasitic absorption loss and a fixed band gap, limiting device optimization. A novel, tunable, and wide-bandgap buffer layer deposited with high conformality is needed to improve carrier collection and reduce interface recombination in these polycrystalline thin-film devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a precisely engineered ZrS2 buffer layer, deposited via atomic layer deposition (ALD), will provide superior band alignment with the CZTSSe absorber, reduce interface defect density, and enable higher open-circuit voltage and efficiency compared to standard CdS buffers.
proposals.method: We will develop a thermal ALD process for ZrS2 using tetrakis(dimethylamido)zirconium(IV) (TDMAZr) and H2S as precursors. The process parameters (temperature, pulse/purge times) will be optimized to achieve stoichiometric, crystalline ZrS2 films on CZTSSe absorbers. The band gap and conduction band position of the ZrS2 will be tuned by controlling the ALD cycle number and post-deposition annealing. Complete solar cells with the structure SLG/Mo/CZTSSe/ALD-ZrS2/i-ZnO/ZnO:Al will be fabricated and compared to CdS-buffered reference devices.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first ALD process for ZrS2 as a photovoltaic buffer layer and demonstrate its integration into CZTSSe devices. It will provide a fundamental understanding of the ZrS2/CZTSSe interface properties and deliver a new, scalable pathway to mitigate voltage losses in kesterite photovoltaics, contributing directly to the goal of pushing efficiencies beyond 15%.
proposals.novelty: While ALD is known for oxides, its application to deposit crystalline metal sulfide buffers like ZrS2 for chalcogenide solar cells is unexplored. The approach combines the conformality and precision of ALD with the novel electronic properties of ZrS2 to create a tunable interface layer, moving beyond the empirical 'trial-and-error' of traditional chemical bath deposition.

proposals.title: Direct, Buffer-Free Heterojunction Formation for CZTSSe Solar Cells via In-Situ Surface Passivation and Band Alignment Engineering
proposals.motivation: The CdS buffer layer in CZTSSe solar cells introduces parasitic absorption in the blue wavelength region and creates a conduction band offset that can limit carrier collection, contributing to the persistent voltage deficit. While incremental modifications to the CdS/CZTSSe interface have been explored, a radical departure from this default architecture is needed to unlock higher efficiencies. This work directly addresses the challenge of interface recombination without the constraints of a traditional n-type buffer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully engineered, chemically clean CZTSSe absorber surface can be directly coupled to a high-transparency window layer (e.g., Zn(O,S)) without a discrete buffer, by using in-situ surface treatments to create a favorable band alignment and suppress interface recombination.
proposals.method: We will fabricate CZTSSe absorbers using a Li-enhanced liquid-phase-assisted grain growth process to achieve large, high-quality grains. Prior to window layer deposition, the absorber surface will undergo controlled treatments including (1) a low-power oxygen plasma for surface oxide formation, (2) a brief ammonium sulfide ((NH4)2S) etch for sulfur termination, and (3) atomic layer deposition (ALD) of an ultra-thin (<5 nm) Zn(O,S) layer with graded oxygen-to-sulfur ratio to tune the conduction band offset. The full device will be completed with i-ZnO/ITO. Advanced characterization, including temperature-dependent J-V, C-V, and DLCP, will be used to analyze interface recombination and band alignment.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, buffer-free heterojunction architecture for kesterite photovoltaics, potentially increasing short-circuit current by reducing parasitic absorption. It will provide a fundamental understanding of the surface chemistry and electronic structure required for a high-quality direct interface, moving beyond the paradigm of simply replacing CdS with another compound buffer layer.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in abandoning the discrete buffer layer concept entirely, instead using in-situ surface passivation and a graded ALD window layer to engineer the heterojunction directly on the CZTSSe grain surface, a significant departure from the incremental cation substitution or thickness optimization approaches prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Atomic-Layer-Deposited Zn(O,S) and ZnSnO Buffer Layers for Enhanced Band Alignment and Reduced Interface Recombination in CZTSSe Solar Cells
proposals.motivation: Despite progress in CZTSSe absorber quality, power conversion efficiencies remain far below the Shockley-Queisser limit, largely due to non-ideal band alignment and high interface recombination at the critical absorber/buffer heterojunction. The conventional CdS buffer layer suffers from parasitic absorption and a conduction band offset (CBO) that is often detrimental, creating a major bottleneck for further efficiency gains as highlighted in pre-2026 Solar Cell Efficiency Tables.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing the CdS buffer with wide-bandgap, tunable materials like Zn(O,S) and ZnSnO, deposited via atomic layer deposition (ALD), will create a more favorable 'spike-like' CBO with the CZTSSe absorber, thereby suppressing interface recombination and improving open-circuit voltage (Voc) and fill factor (FF).
proposals.method: We will fabricate baseline CZTSSe devices using a Li-enhanced liquid-phase-assisted grain growth process. ALD will be used to deposit Zn(O,S) and ZnSnO buffer layers with precise thickness and composition control (varying O/S ratio, Zn/Sn ratio). Device performance will be characterized by J-V and EQE measurements. The band alignment and interface chemistry will be investigated using XPS/UPS for band offsets and advanced micro-to-atom scale characterizations (TEM, EELS) to correlate interfacial atomic structure with electronic properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a clear materials-property-performance relationship for novel buffer layers in CZTSSe photovoltaics, providing a pathway to overcome the Voc deficit. It will deliver a processed, non-toxic alternative to CdS that is compatible with high-efficiency device architecture, directly contributing to higher efficiency entries in future solar cell efficiency tables.
proposals.novelty: While Zn(O,S) has been studied for CIGS, its application to CZTSSe with ALD precision and the exploration of ZnSnO as a buffer are not well-established. Combining these novel buffers with state-of-the-art, Li-treated CZTSSe absorbers to engineer the interface at the atomic scale represents a significant, non-obvious advancement.

proposals.title: Molecular Passivation of Ionic Defects in Kesterite CZTSSe Thin Films: A Transfer from Perovskite Photovoltaics
proposals.motivation: Kesterite (CZTSSe) solar cells suffer from severe open-circuit voltage deficits, primarily due to high non-radiative recombination at grain boundaries and surfaces from ionic point defects (e.g., Cu_Zn, Zn_Cu, V_Se). This limits efficiency well below the theoretical Shockley-Queisser limit. Concurrently, perovskite solar cells have achieved remarkable efficiency gains through molecular defect passivation, a strategy largely unexplored in kesterite photovoltaics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted molecular passivators, specifically Lewis acids (e.g., PbI2, SnI4) and Lewis bases (e.g., thiophene derivatives, pyridine), which successfully pacify ionic defects in perovskites, will bind to and neutralize charged defect states on CZTSSe surfaces and grain boundaries, thereby reducing recombination and enhancing device performance.
proposals.method: We will synthesize CZTSSe absorbers via a standard solution process. A library of perovskite-derived passivators will be applied via spin-coating post-annealing. Advanced micro-to-atom scale characterizations (TEM, XPS, Raman) will map defect chemistry and passivator bonding. Device performance will be evaluated via J-V, EQE, and time-resolved photoluminescence. The correlation between specific passivator chemistry, defect density (via DLTS), and carrier collection efficiency will be quantitatively established.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel materials chemistry toolkit for kesterite defect engineering, directly targeting the voltage-limiting defect states. It is expected to yield a measurable reduction in interface recombination and a pathway to higher open-circuit voltages, contributing to closing the efficiency gap for kesterite solar cells as documented in the Solar Cell Efficiency Tables.
proposals.novelty: While defect passivation is common in silicon and perovskites, the systematic application of perovskite-specific molecular passivation chemistry to the distinct ionic defect landscape of kesterites is a novel, cross-material-class approach. It moves beyond traditional elemental doping (e.g., Ge, Li) to targeted surface and grain boundary molecular engineering.

proposals.title: In-situ TEM Observation of Grain Growth Dynamics and Interface Evolution During Kesterite (CZTSSe) Annealing
proposals.motivation: Kesterite solar cells suffer from low open-circuit voltage, largely attributed to defects and secondary phases formed during the critical selenization/sulfurization process. Pre-2026, post-annealing characterization provides only static snapshots, obscuring the dynamic pathways of grain growth and phase evolution that dictate final film quality. Understanding these real-time dynamics is essential to rationally control the process and improve device efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in-situ TEM will reveal that grain growth and secondary phase formation during kesterite selenization occur via specific, kinetically limited pathways, and that the introduction of alkali elements (Li, Na) modifies these pathways by enhancing liquid-phase-assisted growth and suppressing detrimental phase segregation.
proposals.method: We will adapt in-situ TEM heating and gas-cell techniques from battery research (e.g., for observing solid-electrolyte interphase formation) to kesterite processing. Precursor stacks (e.g., sputtered metal layers) will be deposited directly onto MEMS-based heating chips. Using a Protochips Atmosphere system, we will introduce H2Se or S vapor while heating to 500-550°C inside the TEM column. We will perform real-time imaging (bright-field/dark-field TEM) and selected-area electron diffraction to track grain nucleation, growth, and phase transformations. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy and electron energy loss spectroscopy will be used intermittently to monitor composition changes at interfaces.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, mechanistic view of the dynamic microstructural evolution during kesterite formation, linking specific processing conditions (temperature, Se/S pressure, alkali doping) to critical microstructural outcomes. The findings will establish a foundational understanding to guide the optimization of annealing recipes, directly addressing the voltage deficit problem in CZTSSe photovoltaics.
proposals.novelty: While in-situ TEM is established in battery and catalysis research, its application to the complex, gas-solid reaction of kesterite selenization is novel. This approach moves beyond post-mortem analysis to directly probe the transient phases and interfacial reactions that have been major sources of speculation but never directly observed.

proposals.title: Enhancing Effective Carrier Lifetime in CZTSSe Solar Cells via Photon Recycling and Dielectric Back Reflectors
proposals.motivation: CZTSSe solar cells suffer from a high open-circuit voltage deficit, largely attributed to non-radiative recombination and short minority carrier lifetimes. Photon recycling, a key mechanism in high-efficiency III-V cells like GaAs, can boost the effective carrier lifetime by re-absorbing internally emitted photons, but its implementation in polycrystalline kesterite films remains largely unexplored. Applying advanced optical management to CZTSSe could address a core efficiency bottleneck, moving the technology closer to its theoretical potential.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a high-quality, low-loss dielectric back reflector (DBR) into the CZTSSe device stack will significantly enhance photon recycling, thereby increasing the effective minority carrier lifetime and reducing the open-circuit voltage deficit.
proposals.method: We will fabricate standard CZTSSe solar cells via a sequential process and deposit a tailored DBR (e.g., alternating layers of SiO2 and TiO2) between the Mo back contact and the absorber. Optical modeling using finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations will optimize the DBR design for maximum reflectivity within the CZTSSe luminescence spectrum. The impact on photon recycling will be quantified through comparative measurements of external radiative efficiency (via photoluminescence quantum yield) and effective carrier lifetime (via time-resolved photoluminescence decay) on devices with and without the DBR.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental demonstration and quantitative analysis of photon recycling in CZTSSe solar cells. It will establish a direct link between engineered optical structures and improved carrier lifetime, offering a new pathway to reduce voltage losses. The findings will contribute a novel device architecture to the kesterite research field, complementing existing efforts focused on grain growth and bulk defect passivation.
proposals.novelty: While DBRs are common in III-V photovoltaics, their application to enhance photon recycling in solution-processed, polycrystalline CZTSSe is novel. This approach uniquely targets the optical environment to mitigate electronic losses, a strategy distinct from the predominant chemical and defect-engineering methods in kesterite research.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Co-Design of Monolithic Tandem Photovoltaics: Integrating Scalable Kesterite Top Cells with Low-Temperature Bottom Cells
proposals.motivation: Tandem solar cells are essential for surpassing the single-junction efficiency limit, yet their commercial potential is hindered by sequential development that neglects manufacturability. Most high-efficiency demonstrations rely on materials or processes incompatible with low-cost, large-scale production, such as high-temperature treatments that damage underlying layers. A systematic co-design methodology is urgently needed to identify tandem pairs optimized for both performance and scalable fabrication.
proposals.hypothesis: By concurrently screening candidate bottom-cell materials (e.g., perovskite, organic) against scalable, low-temperature-processed kesterite (CZTSSe) top cells, we can identify monolithic tandem architectures that achieve >30% efficiency while maintaining compatibility with industrially viable deposition and interconnection sequences.
proposals.method: We will establish a high-throughput experimental pipeline using combinatorial inkjet printing and rapid thermal processing to fabricate CZTSSe top cells with varied Ge/Li doping (leveraging our recent grain growth advances) on diverse bottom-cell substrates. A parallel computational workflow, using density functional theory and device physics simulations (e.g., SCAPS-1D), will screen for optimal bandgap pairing and interfacial stability. Critical integration steps—low-temperature transparent conductive oxide deposition and robust tunnel junction formation—will be iteratively optimized within the same pipeline, with stability assessed under ISOS protocols.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly accessible database of co-designed tandem material pairs and integration protocols, validated with prototype devices. It will deliver a novel, patent-protected fabrication sequence for a monolithic CZTSSe-based tandem, demonstrating a clear pathway to a levelized cost of electricity lower than silicon photovoltaics.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its holistic, system-level screening, which explicitly prioritizes scalable process compatibility from the outset, moving beyond the typical focus on standalone material efficiency. It uniquely applies high-throughput methods to the integration challenge itself, particularly for kesterite-based tandems which have seen limited monolithic development.

proposals.title: Spectrally-Staged Tandem Photovoltaics: A Dynamic Interlayer Architecture for Photon Energy Management Inspired by Rocket Staging
proposals.motivation: Tandem solar cells are the primary pathway to surpass the single-junction Shockley-Queisser limit, but their efficiency is constrained by thermalization and transmission losses from imperfect spectral splitting. Current monolithic tandems rely on static bandgap combinations and tunnel junctions, which impose strict current-matching requirements and limit material choices. A new paradigm for dynamic, spectrally-adaptive management is needed to fundamentally reduce these losses and unlock new high-efficiency architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: By implementing a dynamic, reconfigurable 'photonic stage' that selectively captures and then becomes transparent to a targeted high-energy photon band, we can decouple the spectral management from the static bandgap of the subcell, thereby reducing thermalization losses and enabling more flexible, high-performance tandem designs.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a proof-of-concept two-terminal tandem with a dynamic top 'stage.' This stage integrates a wide-bandgap CZTSSe absorber, leveraging our expertise in kesterite grain growth and Ge alloying for bandgap tuning (~1.8 eV). A key innovation is a Li-enhanced, liquid-phase-processed interlayer that undergoes a reversible, thermally or electrically triggered phase change. This stage will first act as a high-energy photon absorber. Upon saturation or via an external trigger, it will transition to a transparent state, 'jettisoning' the lower-energy spectrum to an optimized silicon bottom cell. Advanced characterization (e.g., in-situ UV-Vis, TRPL) will monitor the dynamic spectral transmittance and carrier dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental demonstration of a dynamically reconfigurable spectral filter integrated into a working tandem cell, moving beyond static spectrum splitting. It will deliver a new framework for analyzing tandem cell losses through the lens of staging efficiency and establish a novel materials processing route for phase-change photonic layers in photovoltaics.
proposals.novelty: The direct analogy to multi-stage rocket design, translating the concept of 'jettisoning dead weight' into a dynamically transparent photovoltaic absorber, is a fundamentally new approach to tandem cell architecture. It departs from the paradigm of permanent, current-matched junctions.

proposals.title: Intrinsic Diagnostic Structures for Unambiguous Efficiency Validation in CZTSSe-Perovskite Tandem Solar Cells
proposals.motivation: The rapid rise of perovskite-based tandems, including with kesterite (CZTSSe) bottom cells, has created a reproducibility crisis. As reported in Solar Cell Efficiency Tables, efficiency claims are often ambiguous due to the lack of standardized, reliable methods to deconvolve sub-cell performance under real spectra. This hinders scientific progress and commercial confidence. The field urgently needs a paradigm shift from post-fabrication characterization to co-designed, intrinsic measurement.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding standardized test structures and optical/electrical access points directly into the monolithic tandem architecture during fabrication, we can achieve rigorous, protocol-defined separation of sub-cell current-voltage characteristics and spectral response, leading to irrefutably validated and directly comparable efficiency reports.
proposals.method: We will co-design a 2-terminal monolithic CZTSSe/perovskite tandem cell with integrated lateral test pads connected to each sub-cell via laser-patterned isolation, enabling individual sub-cell J-V measurement. The design will include monolithically integrated spectral sensors (miniature filtered photodiodes) adjacent to the active area to monitor in-situ spectral irradiance. A standardized measurement protocol, compatible with IEC 60904 standards, will be developed using these built-in features to correct for spectral mismatch and report stabilized maximum power point efficiency for each sub-cell and the full tandem.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new class of tandem solar cells with built-in, universally accepted validation capabilities, moving the field beyond contested efficiency records. It will provide a concrete hardware and protocol blueprint for reporting tandem efficiencies that are transparent, reproducible, and directly comparable across laboratories, accelerating the development of high-efficiency, stable tandems.
proposals.novelty: While diagnostic structures exist in silicon PV, their integration as a co-design element in solution-processed thin-film tandems for standardized measurement is novel. This approach fundamentally rethinks the device architecture to serve dual purposes: power generation and intrinsic, standardized self-characterization.

proposals.title: A Viability-First Computational Pipeline for the Discovery of Stable and Sustainable Quantum Dot and Organic Photovoltaic Absorbers
proposals.motivation: The efficiency of emerging photovoltaic (PV) technologies, such as quantum dots (QDs) and organic semiconductors, has advanced significantly, yet their commercial viability is often limited by poor environmental stability, reliance on scarce/toxic elements, and complex synthesis. This creates a 'valley of death' between lab-scale efficiency records and practical deployment. A discovery paradigm that prioritizes intrinsic stability and processability from the outset is urgently needed to identify materials with genuine translational potential.
proposals.hypothesis: By employing a computational screening filter based on stability, sustainability, and synthetic accessibility descriptors prior to experimental investigation, we can efficiently identify quantum dot and organic absorber candidates that inherently possess the foundational properties required for practical solar cell application.
proposals.method: We will develop a high-throughput computational pipeline using density functional theory (DFT) calculations and cheminformatics tools. Stability will be assessed via formation energy and decomposition enthalpy calculations. Elemental sustainability will be scored using the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index for crustal abundance. Synthetic accessibility will be evaluated using fragment-based complexity scores. Candidate libraries (e.g., perovskite-inspired structures, non-fullerene acceptors) will be screened, and only top-ranked materials will be synthesized. Lab synthesis will employ solution-processable methods, with characterization focusing on phase stability under damp heat and illumination, followed by device fabrication and testing.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a curated, open-access dataset of pre-vetted, high-viability absorber candidates for the PV community. It will establish a new materials discovery workflow that explicitly balances efficiency targets with practical constraints, accelerating the development of commercially relevant PV technologies. The project will also yield prototype solar cells from the most promising discovered materials, demonstrating the pipeline's effectiveness.
proposals.novelty: While computational screening for PV absorbers typically focuses on optoelectronic properties (band gap, effective mass), this approach inverts the paradigm by screening first for stability and processability, making efficiency the secondary optimization target. This viability-first filter is a significant departure from standard high-throughput discovery in photovoltaics.

proposals.title: Closed-Loop Spectral Metrology for High-Yield Manufacturing of Monolithic Perovskite-Silicon Tandem Solar Modules
proposals.motivation: The efficiency gap between champion perovskite-silicon tandem cells (>33%) and production modules remains unacceptably large, primarily due to non-uniformities in layer thickness and composition, and interfacial defects that arise during scale-up. Current manufacturing relies on ex-situ characterization, which is too slow for process correction. This project addresses the critical need for in-line, real-time metrology to translate lab-scale tandem cell architectures into high-performance, commercially viable modules, a key challenge identified in pre-2026 solar cell efficiency roadmaps.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating in-situ spectroscopic ellipsometry into a closed-loop control system for tandem module deposition will actively suppress layer non-uniformity and interfacial defects, thereby minimizing scaling losses and enabling the direct transfer of champion cell optoelectronic structures to full-sized modules with high yield.
proposals.method: We will integrate a multi-angle spectroscopic ellipsometer directly into a pilot-scale deposition line for monolithic perovskite-on-silicon tandem modules. Real-time ellipsometry data will be fed into a control algorithm (e.g., a model-predictive controller) that dynamically adjusts deposition parameters (e.g., source rates, substrate temperature) for each layer. The system will be calibrated using reference layers characterized by pre-2026 techniques like TEM and XRD, as used in CZTS studies. The feedback loop will be validated by fabricating minimodules, comparing their layer properties and final performance against statically processed controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, closed-loop manufacturing framework that directly tackles the primary sources of efficiency loss in tandem module scale-up. It will provide the photovoltaic community with a concrete methodology to bridge the lab-to-fab gap, moving beyond passive monitoring to active process control. The resulting high-yield process protocol will be a critical step toward the cost-effective production of ultra-high-efficiency tandem solar panels.
proposals.novelty: While spectroscopic ellipsometry is used for lab characterization, its integration as a real-time, closed-loop control sensor for the dynamic optimization of a full tandem stack during module-scale deposition is a novel manufacturing paradigm. This moves beyond the 'deposit-then-measure' approach that dominates pre-2026 scale-up efforts for next-generation photovoltaics.

proposals.title: Micro-Concentrator Photovoltaic Systems Based on High-Grain-Quality CZTSSe for Ultra-Compact Power Generation
proposals.motivation: Concentrator photovoltaics (CPV) traditionally use large, expensive III-V cells and complex tracking systems, limiting their application. A shift to micro-scale concentrator systems (micro-CPV) using thin-film absorbers like CZTSSe could enable ultra-compact, low-profile power modules for building-integrated PV, portable electronics, and IoT devices. However, the performance of CZTSSe under concentrated light is underexplored, and its grain structure is critical for handling the increased current densities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating Li-enhanced liquid-phase-assisted grain growth to create large, high-quality CZTSSe grains with reduced defect density, and pairing these cells with micro-optical concentrators (e.g., lens arrays), we can achieve a significant efficiency boost under low-to-moderate concentration (2-50 suns) while maintaining a minimal system footprint.
proposals.method: We will fabricate CZTSSe solar cells using a process that incorporates Li-doping to enhance liquid-phase-assisted grain growth, aiming for grains >5 µm. These cells will be characterized via advanced micro-to-atom scale techniques (e.g., TEM, EBSD) to correlate grain and interface quality with performance. Micro-concentrator prototypes will be built by integrating sub-millimeter CZTSSe cells with polymer or glass micro-lens arrays designed using ray-tracing simulations (e.g., Zemax) to achieve uniform illumination. The systems will be tested under standard and concentrated AM1.5G spectra to measure efficiency, carrier collection, and thermal management.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first performance benchmarks for CZTSSe in a micro-CPV architecture, providing new insights into the material's behavior under concentration. It will deliver a prototype demonstrating a pathway to >20% system efficiency at a fraction of the size and material cost of conventional CPV, advancing the field of miniaturized, high-power-density photovoltaics.
proposals.novelty: While CZTSSe grain engineering and micro-CPV are separate pre-2026 research threads, their combination is novel. The specific application of Li-enhanced grain growth, proven for standard illumination, to create cells robust under concentrated light for micro-optics integration represents a significant, non-obvious advancement in both CZTSSe and CPV technology.

proposals.title: Quantifying Intrinsic Loss Limits in Kesterite Solar Cells via Critically Coupled Photonic 'Black Hole' Structures
proposals.motivation: For emerging thin-film photovoltaic materials like CZTSSe, the efficiency gap to the Shockley-Queisser limit is largely attributed to poorly quantified intrinsic loss channels, such as tail-state recombination, bandgap fluctuations, and parasitic absorption. Existing characterization methods struggle to isolate and measure these losses under conditions of maximized optical generation. This project aims to create a novel experimental platform to directly probe these ultimate material limits.
proposals.hypothesis: By designing nanophotonic structures that force near-total absorption of incident light into specific, known loss pathways within a PV material, we can achieve a controlled, maximized-loss state that allows for the precise spectroscopic quantification of intrinsic non-radiative recombination coefficients and parasitic absorption cross-sections.
proposals.method: We will fabricate thin-film CZTSSe devices integrated with photonic crystal slab cavities or guided resonance structures engineered for critical coupling. Finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations will optimize geometries to maximize absorption in the CZTSSe layer while minimizing useful carrier extraction, intentionally enhancing loss pathways. Advanced characterization—including photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) mapping, transient absorption spectroscopy, and temperature-dependent J-V analysis under high injection—will be performed on these 'black hole' devices. The data will be used to fit and quantify parameters like Auger coefficients and trap densities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental dataset on the ultimate loss limits in kesterite solar cells under conditions of perfect optical absorption. It will establish a new methodology for loss quantification applicable to other PV material systems, informing targeted material engineering strategies to suppress dominant loss channels.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the core objective of photovoltaics—maximizing useful absorption—to instead maximize and study loss, a concept adapted from acoustic perfect absorbers but not previously applied to quantify PV material limits. It moves beyond modeling to create a controlled experimental platform for loss spectroscopy.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Quantum Dot and Organic Solar Cells as Spectroscopic Probes for Loss Channel Identification
proposals.motivation: Emerging quantum dot (QD) and organic photovoltaic (OPV) materials offer tunable optoelectronic properties but suffer from complex, intertwined loss mechanisms that limit efficiency, as seen in the analogous challenges for kesterite CZTSSe. Traditional characterization often correlates losses with structure but struggles to establish direct causality. A method to rank and definitively identify the most detrimental loss channels is critically needed to guide material synthesis and device engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: By deliberately designing QD and OPV device architectures to minimize photocurrent output through controlled structural perturbations, the resulting efficiency losses can be directly and causally mapped to specific fundamental loss mechanisms, providing a clear hierarchy of their impact.
proposals.method: We will employ a computational inverse design framework using finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) optical simulations and drift-diffusion charge transport modeling to generate device structures with predicted minimal efficiency. Key designed perturbations include graded QD ligand densities to control inter-dot coupling, tailored Gaussian density-of-states for simulating energy disorder in OPVs, and engineered interfacial dipole layers. These designed 'minimally efficient' structures will be fabricated (e.g., PbS QD and PBDB-T:ITIC OPV systems) and characterized using advanced techniques like transient photovoltage and photoluminescence spectroscopy to correlate specific losses (non-radiative recombination, poor collection, parasitic absorption) with each designed flaw.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new causative spectroscopy that directly ranks loss mechanisms by impact, moving beyond correlation. It will produce a validated design rule set of 'anti-structures' to avoid in next-generation QD and OPV synthesis. The methodology provides a generalizable framework for loss analysis applicable to other emerging PV materials, such as perovskites and kesterites.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the standard efficiency maximization paradigm, using deliberately poor devices as precise diagnostic tools. It synthesizes inverse design computational methods—typically used for performance optimization—with a spectroscopic goal to deconvolute losses, a novel synthesis not previously applied to these material systems.

proposals.title: A Closed-Loop, Machine-Learning-Driven Roll-to-Roll Platform for Accelerated Perovskite Solar Cell Process Optimization
proposals.motivation: Despite perovskite solar cells achieving certified lab efficiencies exceeding 25%, a significant lab-to-fab gap persists. Roll-to-roll (R2R) manufacturing, crucial for low-cost deployment, suffers from complex, interdependent process parameters that are slow and expensive to optimize manually. This project addresses the critical need to rapidly translate champion lab results into stable, high-performance manufactured films to enable commercial viability.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a real-time literature mining and validation system with an adaptive R2R pilot line will create a closed-loop platform capable of autonomously discovering and implementing optimal processing conditions, thereby accelerating the optimization cycle and improving the performance and reproducibility of manufactured perovskite films.
proposals.method: We will develop a natural language processing (NLP) pipeline to automatically extract material compositions, deposition, and annealing parameters from newly published perovskite literature. This data will train a Bayesian optimization algorithm to propose high-potential parameter sets. These will be executed on a modular R2R line equipped with in-situ photoluminescence and XRD for immediate film quality feedback. The resulting performance data (efficiency, stability) will be fed back to refine the optimization model, creating a self-improving loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, adaptive manufacturing platform that directly bridges academic research and industrial production. It will generate a unique, high-quality dataset linking R2R process parameters to film properties and device performance, providing fundamental insights into scalable perovskite processing. The project aims to demonstrate a significant reduction in the time and cost required to achieve target efficiency and stability benchmarks on flexible substrates.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning is used for materials discovery and R2R for deposition, the closed-loop integration of automated literature mining with real-time, adaptive physical manufacturing for autonomous process optimization is a novel and transformative approach. It moves beyond static design-of-experiments to a dynamic system that learns from the global research frontier.

proposals.title: Standardized Test Structures for Isolating and Benchmarking Sub-Cell Performance in Kesterite and Related Thin-Film Photovoltaics
proposals.motivation: The efficiency of thin-film solar cells like CZTSSe is limited by complex, coupled losses at grain boundaries, interfaces, and within transport layers. Current diagnostic methods, such as full-device J-V and EQE, convolute these effects, making it difficult to isolate and quantify the performance of individual components. This hinders systematic optimization and the development of predictive models for next-generation materials.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-fabricating dedicated photovoltaic test structures—analogous to van der Pauw or Kelvin bridges in microelectronics—alongside full solar cells, we can decouple and quantitatively benchmark the intrinsic efficiency of key sub-cell components, such as carrier extraction across a specific interface or the conductivity of a doped grain boundary network.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate test structures on CZTSSe and related absorber substrates to isolate critical elements: 1) lateral structures with selective contacts to measure carrier extraction efficiency of holes/electrons from the absorber, 2) symmetric contact stacks to quantify specific contact resistivity via transfer length method (TLM), and 3) defined grain boundary networks (via seed layers or patterning) to assess recombination velocity. These will be characterized using calibrated, intensity-dependent photoconductance and photoluminescence, with data analyzed using established semiconductor device physics models to extract standardized metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will create a foundational library of standardized performance data for photovoltaic sub-components, enabling precise diagnosis of loss mechanisms. It will provide a new methodology for the community to benchmark transport layers and interfaces independently of the full device, accelerating targeted optimization and materials development for kesterite and related emerging photovoltaic technologies.
proposals.novelty: While test structures are standard in microelectronics, their systematic adaptation to decouple and benchmark the *photovoltaic* efficiency of sub-cell components (e.g., an interface's carrier extraction efficiency) within a thin-film solar cell fabrication process is a novel and powerful diagnostic approach.

proposals.title: Direct Contact Strategies for CZTSSe Solar Cells: Investigating the Necessity of the MnS Buffer Layer for Schottky Junction Elimination
proposals.motivation: Kesterite CZTSSe solar cells, a promising non-toxic and earth-abundant thin-film technology, are limited by low open-circuit voltage (Voc), partly attributed to recombination at the heterojunction interface. The recent introduction of an MnS buffer layer has shown promise in reducing interface recombination and improving efficiency. However, its role in forming a specific band alignment that eliminates the detrimental Schottky junction with the Mo back contact remains unclear and adds process complexity. Determining if this layer is fundamentally essential, or if alternative contact strategies can achieve the same electronic effect, is critical for simplifying fabrication and pushing efficiencies closer to the theoretical limit.
proposals.hypothesis: The primary function of the MnS layer is to engineer a specific band alignment at the CZTSSe/Mo interface, and that this Schottky barrier elimination can be replicated or improved using alternative, simpler material stacks or direct contact modifications, without compromising device performance.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a series of CZTSSe solar cells using a co-evaporation/selenization process, systematically varying the back contact structure. Control devices will use the standard Mo/MnS stack. Experimental sets will employ: (1) direct CZTSSe deposition on Mo, (2) Mo pre-treated with thin metallic (e.g., Ti, W) or compound (e.g., MoO_x, MoN) interlayers deposited by sputtering, and (3) alternative buffer materials (e.g., ZnS, SnS) replacing MnS. Devices will be characterized by current-voltage (J-V), capacitance-voltage (C-V), and temperature-dependent J-V to extract diode parameters and Schottky barrier heights. Advanced micro-to-atom scale characterization (EDS, TEM, XPS) from the researcher's recent work will be used to correlate interfacial chemistry and microstructure with electronic performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive understanding of the electronic role of the MnS buffer, distinguishing between its function as a mere diffusion barrier and an essential band alignment layer. It will establish a materials selection framework for back contact engineering in kesterite photovoltaics. The project aims to identify a simplified, high-performance contact stack, contributing directly to the roadmap for achieving >15% efficient CZTSSe devices as tracked in the Solar Cell Efficiency Tables.
proposals.novelty: While recent work has focused on optimizing the MnS layer itself, this proposal challenges its fundamental necessity by exploring a wide design space of alternative interlayers and direct contact strategies informed by band alignment principles, a systematic approach not yet reported in the literature. It uniquely combines the researcher's expertise in advanced grain growth (e.g., Li-enhanced processes) and nanoscale characterization to solve a critical interface problem.

proposals.title: Interface Engineering of MnS/ITO Contacts for Reduced Schottky Barrier Losses in High-Efficiency CZTSSe Solar Cells
proposals.motivation: Kesterite CZTSSe solar cells, a promising non-toxic and earth-abundant thin-film photovoltaic technology, are limited by significant open-circuit voltage (Voc) deficits. Recent work, including Dr. Green's on Li-enhanced grain growth, has improved bulk quality, but non-ideal carrier collection at the rear contact remains a critical bottleneck. Specifically, the MnS/ITO interface, commonly used as a back contact, is suspected to form a Schottky barrier, increasing series resistance and recombination, thereby capping efficiency below the theoretical limit.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Schottky barrier at the MnS/ITO interface is primarily caused by Fermi-level pinning due to interface states and a large work function mismatch, and that targeted interfacial modification can reduce the barrier height, leading to improved fill factor and Voc.
proposals.method: We will fabricate CZTSSe devices with a standard MnS/ITO back contact structure. The interface will be modified via: 1) Ultra-thin (<5 nm) elemental interlayers (e.g., Ti, Mo) deposited by ALD prior to ITO sputtering to tune the effective work function. 2) A low-temperature plasma treatment (N2, O2) of the MnS surface to modify its electronic properties. Device performance will be characterized by J-V and EQE. The interface will be studied via XPS for chemical states and UPS for work function/band alignment. Temperature-dependent J-V analysis will quantitatively extract the Schottky barrier height.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, mechanistic understanding of loss pathways at the MnS/ITO contact in state-of-the-art kesterite cells. It will deliver a practical processing strategy to reduce contact resistance, directly contributing to higher efficiency devices as tracked in the 'Solar Cell Efficiency Tables'. The findings will establish general guidelines for back-contact engineering in chalcogenide photovoltaics.
proposals.novelty: While bulk and buffer layer optimization in CZTSSe is well-studied, systematic analysis and engineering of the specific MnS/ITO Schottky interface is novel. The approach combines precise ALD interlayer control with direct electronic characterization of the interface, moving beyond empirical contact optimization.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop, Self-Optimizing Roll-to-Roll Perovskite Manufacturing
ideas.description: This project integrates an automated data collection and validation system for perovskite solar cell efficiency records directly into a roll-to-roll (R2R) manufacturing pilot line. The system continuously ingests and analyzes published lab-scale processing details and performance claims, identifying promising parameter combinations. These insights are then used to automatically propose and test adjustments to the R2R deposition parameters—such as coating speed, annealing temperature, or precursor composition—in a controlled manner. This creates a self-optimizing production platform that can rapidly adapt to the latest research breakthroughs, accelerating the translation of champion lab efficiencies into stable, high-performance manufactured films.

ideas.title: Closed-loop spectral metrology for scaling of high-efficiency tandem cell architectures
ideas.description: This research integrates in-line spectroscopic ellipsometry directly into the scale-up process for next-generation tandem solar cells (e.g., perovskite-silicon). By providing real-time, nanometer-precise control of each layer's thickness, composition, and interface quality during the deposition of full-sized modules, it moves beyond simple process monitoring. The synergy enables the creation of a closed-loop manufacturing system where ellipsometry data is used to dynamically adjust deposition parameters, actively combating the primary sources of scaling losses—such as layer non-uniformity and interfacial defects—that degrade champion cell performance. This approach allows for the first time the direct translation of lab-optimized optoelectronic structures into high-yield production modules with minimized efficiency gaps.

ideas.title: Designing Photonic 'Black Holes' to Maximize and Quantify Intrinsic Photovoltaic Loss Channels
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from acoustics, where perfect absorbers are designed to maximize and study dissipation, to the field of photovoltaics. The core idea is to create the inverse of an ideal light-trapping solar cell: instead of maximizing useful absorption, we design nanophotonic 'black hole' structures (e.g., critically coupled cavities, guided resonance traps, or topological light channels) that intentionally and maximally couple incident light into the PV material's known intrinsic loss channels, such as specific non-radiative recombination pathways or parasitic absorption in non-active layers. By engineering structures that achieve near-total absorption with near-zero electrical output, we create a controlled, maximized-loss experimental platform. This allows for the precise spectroscopic isolation and quantification of fundamental loss mechanisms—like Auger recombination, trap-assisted recombination, or parasitic free-carrier absorption—providing direct experimental data on the ultimate efficiency limits imposed by the material itself, scaling these findings across different material systems and device architectures.

ideas.title: Standardized Performance Benchmarks for Sub-Cell Photovoltaic Components via Micro-Electromechanical Systems (MEMS) Calibration Techniques
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from Micro-Electromechanical Systems (MEMS) and Integrated Circuit (IC) testing to the problem of benchmarking sub-cell photovoltaic components. In MEMS/IC fabrication, standardized test structures (e.g., van der Pauw, Kelvin bridges) are co-fabricated on the wafer to isolate and measure the performance of specific materials and process steps, such as contact resistance or layer conductivity. By adapting this approach, we propose designing and fabricating dedicated photovoltaic 'test structures' that isolate individual layers (like transport layers) or interfaces (like contacts) within a device stack. These structures will be used to establish standardized efficiency benchmarks—such as carrier extraction efficiency or interfacial recombination velocity—for these sub-cell components under controlled, calibrated conditions. This creates a foundational library of performance data for individual photovoltaic elements, enabling more precise diagnostics, targeted optimization, and predictive modeling for next-generation solar cell designs.

ideas.title: Co-Design of Tandem Cell Architectures with Intrinsic, Standardized Characterization Protocols
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to co-develop next-generation tandem solar cell architectures alongside novel, intrinsic characterization protocols specifically designed for their complex multi-junction physics. Instead of applying existing measurement standards as an afterthought, the project will embed standardized test structures and diagnostic features directly into the cell and module design from the outset. This will enable rigorous, unambiguous separation of sub-cell performance, accurate measurement of current matching under real spectral conditions, and reliable reporting of stabilized efficiency for perovskite-based and other novel tandems. The outcome will be a new class of tandem devices whose record efficiencies are not only higher but are also irrefutably validated and comparable through built-in, universally accepted measurement capabilities.

ideas.title: Establish a Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)-Driven Verification Protocol for Emerging Thin-Film Photovoltaics
ideas.description: This research direction develops a new, independent verification protocol specifically for emerging thin-film PV technologies (e.g., perovskites, CZTS, organic PV) that prioritizes Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) and cost-per-watt as its core metrics, rather than peak laboratory efficiency. The protocol will define standardized, accelerated testing procedures to measure and report critical economic drivers—such as degradation rates, performance under realistic spectra and temperatures, material utilization, and manufacturability—alongside efficiency. This creates a transparent, comparative benchmark for investors and manufacturers, directly linking material science progress to financial performance and steering the field toward innovations that maximize practical energy yield per dollar, not just peak efficiency.

ideas.title: Quantum-Loss Spectroscopy: Designing Minimally Efficient Quantum Dot and Organic PV Structures to Map Fundamental Loss Channels
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes the inverse design philosophy with the exploration of novel quantum dot and organic photovoltaic materials. We will deliberately engineer nanostructures and heterojunctions within these emerging material systems to create devices with minimized photocurrent output, effectively turning them into tailored spectroscopic tools. By correlating designed structural perturbations—such as controlled interfacial defects, tailored energy disorder, or specific light-scattering geometries—with dramatic reductions in measured efficiency, we can map the fundamental loss channels unique to these material classes. This approach provides a direct, causative method to rank loss mechanisms and identify the most critical material-level bottlenecks, guiding the targeted synthesis of next-generation absorbers by first understanding what to avoid.

ideas.title: Adapting Multi-Stage Rocket Staging to Photon Energy Management in Tandem Cells
ideas.description: This research adapts the problem-solving principle from multi-stage rocket design—where distinct, optimized systems operate sequentially and dead weight is discarded—to the challenge of pushing tandem solar cell efficiencies beyond single-junction limits. The analogous aerospace solution uses staged propulsion systems, each jettisoned after use. Adapted to photovoltaics, the novel architecture involves developing dynamic, reconfigurable interlayers or top cells that act as 'photonic stages.' These stages would be engineered to capture a specific high-energy photon band, then change state (e.g., via a phase-change material or electrochromic filter) to become transparent, effectively 'jettisoning' the unused lower-energy spectrum for optimal conversion by the subsequent cell. This approach aims to more precisely manage the solar spectrum, reducing fundamental losses and enabling new material combinations for ultra-high-efficiency tandem stacks.

ideas.title: A Dynamic Ranking and Selection Engine for High-Throughput Tandem Solar Cell Development
ideas.description: This research direction develops a high-throughput computational-experimental framework where novel tandem cell architectures (e.g., perovskite/silicon, all-perovskite, III-V/Si) are treated as competitors in a dynamic league. An adapted Glicko-2 rating system continuously updates the 'skill' score of each material combination and stacking design based on experimental efficiency, stability, and manufacturability data from iterative testing cycles. The algorithm's uncertainty parameter and predictive match outcomes are then used to intelligently recommend the next highest-potential experiments, prioritizing under-explored but theoretically promising 'dark horse' candidates. This creates a self-optimizing research pipeline that systematically navigates the vast combinatorial space of tandem designs to converge on champion configurations faster than traditional or purely ranking-based approaches.

ideas.title: Co-Design of Scalable Tandem Architectures via High-Throughput Materials and Process Integration Screening
ideas.description: This research program shifts the paradigm from a sequential 'materials-then-integration' approach to a concurrent co-design of the tandem cell as a manufacturable system. It establishes a high-throughput experimental and computational pipeline that screens candidate material combinations (e.g., perovskite-perovskite, perovskite-CIGS, perovskite-organic) not only for spectral matching and theoretical efficiency, but critically for their compatibility with scalable, low-temperature deposition sequences and robust monolithic interconnection. The output is a set of validated, patent-protected tandem architectures paired with specific fabrication protocols that demonstrate >30% efficiency while maintaining a clear pathway to a levelized cost of electricity lower than incumbent single-junction silicon.

ideas.title: Computationally-Guided Discovery of Intrinsically Stable Quantum Dot and Organic PV Absorbers
ideas.description: This research program employs a viability-first discovery pipeline to identify next-generation photovoltaic absorbers with inherently practical potential. A computational screening platform, using established stability and processability descriptors, pre-selects material candidates from quantum dot and organic semiconductor libraries based on their predicted environmental stability, elemental sustainability, and synthetic accessibility. Only the top-ranked candidates from this stability-focused screen are then synthesized in the lab for detailed optoelectronic characterization and device testing. This approach ensures that the search for high efficiency is constrained within a design space of materials that already meet foundational requirements for real-world application, dramatically increasing the translational potential of any discovery.

ideas.title: Co-Designed Quantum Dot/Organic Absorbers for Dynamically Tunable Monolithic Tandem Cells
ideas.description: This research direction focuses on the co-development of novel quantum dot and organic photovoltaic materials specifically engineered for monolithic tandem integration. The core innovation is designing these soft-material absorbers to have externally tunable electronic properties—such as bandgap via quantum confinement modulation or molecular dipole interaction—through applied electric fields, light, or chemical stimuli. This allows a single fabricated tandem device to dynamically reconfigure its current-matching and spectral harvesting profile in response to changes in incident light spectrum, temperature, or desired output. The synergy lies in moving beyond discovering static high-efficiency materials to creating an adaptive, multi-junction system that maximizes energy yield under real-world, non-standard conditions.

ideas.title: Establishing a Standardized Verification Platform for Micro-Concentrator Thin-Film Photovoltaics
ideas.description: This research fuses micro-concentrator system design with independent verification protocols to develop a specialized testing and characterization platform for emerging thin-film cells (e.g., perovskites, CIGS, organic PV) when integrated into micro-optic concentrator architectures. The synergy lies in creating new, standardized performance metrics—such as angular acceptance under concentration, thermal stability in miniaturized packages, and real-world spectral response—that are critical for applications like micro-sensors, drones, and wearable electronics. This platform will accelerate the commercialization of these hybrid systems by providing credible, third-party validated data on efficiency, durability, and energy yield under non-standard operating conditions, a need not addressed by existing silicon-centric or flat-plate thin-film verification programs.

ideas.title: Adapting Slot-Die Coating and Forced Convection from Organic Electronics for Perovskite Roll-to-Roll
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a proven scaling solution from the field of organic electronics to the challenge of perovskite solar cell manufacturing. The analogous problem—scaling solution-processed organic semiconductor films from batch to continuous production—was solved by implementing slot-die coating coupled with in-line forced convection drying. We propose to engineer this integrated system for perovskite deposition. A perovskite precursor ink will be continuously deposited via a slot-die head onto a flexible substrate moving through a roll-to-roll line. Immediately downstream, a controlled forced convection (e.g., air/gas knife, directed IR) zone will be applied to rapidly and uniformly manage solvent removal and initiate crystallization. This adaptation aims to overcome the speed and uniformity bottlenecks of current perovskite coating methods, directly enabling high-throughput, industrially viable manufacturing of perovskite photovoltaic modules.

ideas.title: A Verified Tandem Cell Efficiency Database with Enforced Measurement Protocols
ideas.description: This initiative establishes a central, public database for multi-junction and tandem solar cell efficiencies that is governed by strict, transparent measurement and reporting standards. To submit a record, researchers must provide comprehensive metadata, including spectral irradiance used, current-voltage measurement details, and sub-cell performance data, all conforming to an agreed-upon international protocol. A panel of independent experts reviews high-stakes submissions, and the database interface clearly labels each entry with its verification status (e.g., 'self-reported', 'protocol-compliant', 'independently verified'). This transforms the database from a simple list into a trusted, authoritative tool for tracking genuine technological advancement, enabling fair comparison between different research groups and tandem architectures.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop Accelerated Learning Platform for Predictive Perovskite Solar Cell Stability
ideas.description: This research establishes a dynamic platform that treats stability as a predictive, data-driven science rather than a static materials problem. A high-throughput combinatorial stress testing system subjects perovskite solar cells to synchronized multi-factor stressors (light, heat, humidity, electrical bias) while collecting real-time, in-situ operando degradation data. Machine learning models analyze this data to uncover hidden degradation pathways and predict long-term failure modes. The platform's predictive insights directly and iteratively guide the co-optimization of both material engineering and encapsulation strategies, creating a closed-loop design cycle that proactively builds stability against the complex, synergistic failures encountered in real-world operation.

ideas.title: Co-Develop Dynamic Verification Protocols for Emerging Thin-Film Photovoltaics
ideas.description: This initiative moves beyond applying existing verification to new materials by first building the necessary measurement science foundation. It convenes manufacturers, national labs, and academic experts to co-develop and standardize material-class-specific test protocols for stability and performance, starting with the most promising emerging thin-film types. The system integrates accelerated lifetime testing coupled with real-time, in-situ diagnostics to link observed degradation to physical and chemical mechanisms. This creates a living verification framework where protocols are iteratively refined based on field data and improved scientific understanding, ensuring robust, comparable, and predictive validation for technologies in rapid development.

ideas.title: Constrained Inverse Design for Selective Loss Pathway Amplification
ideas.description: This research employs inverse design under specific constraints to create photovoltaic structures that selectively and maximally exhibit a single, pre-defined fundamental loss mechanism, while keeping others near their optimal performance levels. By computationally enforcing these constraints—for example, maximizing radiative emission while minimizing non-radiative and parasitic absorption—the approach generates clear, interpretable test structures. These fabricated devices allow for the direct experimental probing and quantification of the targeted loss channel in isolation. The method transforms the ill-posed 'minimum efficiency' problem into a set of well-defined experiments that map the contribution of individual loss pathways to overall device physics.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Photovoltaic Discovery with Embedded Accelerated Stability Prognostics
ideas.description: This redesigned approach couples combinatorial material synthesis and automated optoelectrical testing with a parallel, miniaturized accelerated aging pipeline. Material libraries are simultaneously subjected to controlled stress conditions (heat, light, humidity) while their early-stage degradation signatures are measured via in-situ micro-probes. Machine learning models correlate these accelerated aging fingerprints with known long-term stability outcomes from a curated historical dataset, creating a stability prediction score for each new compound. This integrated system enables the high-throughput discovery of photovoltaic materials that are optimized for both initial performance and predicted operational lifetime, effectively de-risking the development pathway.

ideas.title: A Global Tandem Cell Energy Yield Atlas
ideas.description: This initiative creates a unified, open-access database that fuses detailed tandem cell efficiency records with simulated and validated annual energy yield calculations across a global grid of diverse climatic zones. It will track multi-junction and perovskite/silicon tandem architectures not just by their peak laboratory efficiency, but by their predicted and field-verified kWh/m²/year output under specific spectral, temperature, and irradiance conditions. The core innovation is linking each record-holding device's spectral response and temperature coefficients to location-specific climate data, enabling direct performance comparisons and identifying the most economically viable tandem technology for any given region. This shifts the benchmark from a singular laboratory point to a spatially resolved performance landscape, guiding targeted R&D and deployment strategies.

ideas.title: A Physics-Based Standardized Operational Area Framework for Efficiency of Conformable Photovoltaics
ideas.description: This research establishes a robust, comparable efficiency metric for shape-conforming solar cells by solving the reference area problem. It introduces the concept of a Standardized Operational Area (SOA), a dynamically defined but rigorously calculated reference area based on the cell's conformed state geometry and fundamental light-intercepting structure. The work develops measurement protocols and computational tools to determine the SOA for diverse flexible, stretchable, and textile-integrated photovoltaic devices. This framework prevents metric gaming, enables fair technology benchmarking, and directly links device physics to practical energy harvesting potential under real-world deployment scenarios.

ideas.title: Dynamic Real-World Performance Ranking for Photovoltaic Technologies
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a dynamic benchmarking framework for solar cells. It integrates multi-dimensional performance data (under varying spectra, temperature, and irradiance) into a sports-like rating system (e.g., Elo/Glicko). Each real-world condition acts as a unique 'match' or 'tournament' where technologies compete based on efficiency, with their ratings updating to reflect performance reliability across environments. This generates a live, adaptive ranking that identifies not just peak performers, but the most versatile and resilient technologies for global deployment, directly linking lab-characterized real-world performance to an interpretable, competitive metric.

ideas.title: Blockchain-Verified Dynamic Performance Ledger for Photovoltaic Cells
ideas.description: This research proposes a decentralized ledger system that immutably records the performance data of photovoltaic cells tested under a wide spectrum of real-world conditions—such as varying spectra, temperature, and irradiance—rather than just standard test conditions. Each cell or batch receives a unique, timestamped digital certificate on a blockchain, logging its full dynamic performance profile from independent labs. This creates a transparent and tamper-proof history, allowing manufacturers to prove real-world optimization claims and enabling buyers, financiers, and grid operators to verify long-term performance expectations under specific local climates. The synergy transforms subjective or opaque real-world ratings into a secure, standardized, and tradable asset class for the solar industry.

ideas.title: Bayesian Probabilistic Ranking for Photovoltaic Technologies with Uncertainty Quantification
ideas.description: This system dynamically ranks solar cell technologies using a Bayesian framework that explicitly models the uncertainty and experimental context of each reported performance metric. It integrates disparate data points—from controlled lab certifications to field stability studies—weighting them by measurement fidelity and sample size to update a posterior distribution for each technology. The output is a probabilistic ranking with confidence intervals, showing not just which technology is likely best, but also the statistical significance of that lead. This approach is robust to noisy, non-competitive data and provides a clearer picture of technological progress and reliability over time.

ideas.title: A Decentralized, High-Throughput Platform for Rapid Photovoltaic Material Certification
ideas.description: This research proposes a decentralized platform where combinatorial chemistry and automated testing robots, inspired by pharmaceutical screening, generate vast, standardized datasets on new photovoltaic material stability and performance. These datasets are then published in an open, structured format to a cloud-based repository. The core innovation is using this high-volume, high-quality data to develop and benchmark machine learning models that can predict long-term performance from short-term tests. This enables a new certification paradigm where manufacturers can self-certify materials using these validated models, with the global research community crowd-verifying results by auditing the open-source models and contributing their own high-throughput screening data to challenge or confirm predictions.

ideas.title: Rank Photovoltaic Technologies by 'Field Yield' Instead of 'Peak Potential'
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that ranking photovoltaic technologies by record lab-cell efficiency is the best indicator of real-world impact. Drawing an analogy from Agricultural Crop Science, where crop varieties are ranked by average yield across diverse field trials rather than peak experimental plot performance, the solution is adapted. We propose to rank PV technologies by their median production efficiency or 'field yield,' measured through a network of standardized, real-world test installations. This shift would incentivize and highlight technologies that are robust, reliable, and high-performing under typical operating conditions, not just in ideal laboratory settings.

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmaceutical Process Scale-Up to Minimize Photovoltaic Efficiency Loss
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'Quality by Design' (QbD) framework from pharmaceutical manufacturing scale-up to bridge the efficiency gap between record lab cells and commercial photovoltaic modules. The analogous solution involves systematically identifying Critical Process Parameters and Material Attributes that affect final product quality. The adapted idea applies this by defining module efficiency and longevity as Critical Quality Attributes, then using designed experiments and modeling to predict how scaling factors like interconnection stress and encapsulation processes impact these attributes. The goal is to develop a controlled, predictive scale-up process that minimizes efficiency losses by design, rather than through post-production correction, mirroring the approach used to ensure drug efficacy and safety during manufacturing scale-up.

ideas.title: Automated Performance Benchmarking Network for Building-Integrated Hybrid Energy Collectors
ideas.description: This project develops a network of instrumented, dual-purpose photovoltaic-thermal (BIPV/T) collector installations that serve as both energy generators and live data collection nodes. An automated system harvests operational performance data (electrical output, thermal yield, degradation rates) from these field deployments and cross-references it with laboratory efficiency claims and models. This creates a closed-loop validation and discovery platform that identifies performance gaps, optimal installation parameters, and real-world climate-specific efficiency factors. The resulting dynamic, empirically grounded database accelerates the development of next-generation hybrid collectors and provides robust, traceable efficiency benchmarks for architects, engineers, and regulators.

ideas.title: Sub-Cell Component Efficiency Atlas for Module-Scale Loss Diagnostics
ideas.description: This research develops a standardized 'efficiency atlas' of fundamental performance limits for individual cell components (e.g., transport layers, contacts) under ideal lab conditions. This atlas is then used as a reference to systematically diagnose and attribute efficiency losses observed when integrating these components into full-scale production modules. By correlating deviations in component performance from their ideal benchmarks to specific scaling factors (like coating uniformity, interfacial stress, or metallization conductivity over large areas), the work creates a direct, causal link between lab-cell physics and module-level yield. This enables a shift from empirical optimization to a physics-guided, root-cause correction of scaling losses, bridging the efficiency gap with unprecedented precision.

ideas.title: Reliability-Correlated Efficiency Standards for Concentrator Photovoltaics
ideas.description: This research develops new concentrator photovoltaic (CPV) efficiency standards that are explicitly coupled with reliability engineering principles from aerospace. It defines accelerated testing protocols where efficiency is measured not just at initial concentration but throughout stress cycles simulating thermal, mechanical, and spectral field conditions. The core innovation is a failure mode and effects analysis (FMEA) specifically for efficiency loss mechanisms, creating predictive models that correlate short-term performance metrics with long-term degradation. The outcome is a standardized 'lifetime efficiency' rating system that provides a more accurate and bankable prediction of real-world CPV energy production over decades.

ideas.title: A Dynamic, Self-Verifying Database for Photovoltaic Technology Performance
ideas.description: This research proposes the development of an open, dynamic database that ranks emerging photovoltaic technologies not by a single certified record, but by the statistical distribution of performance (e.g., median, variance) from a verified minimum batch size. The core innovation is a tiered credibility protocol, where in-house verification using standardized, rapid characterization tools provides immediate, transparent data entry, which gains weight and visibility as it is validated by subsequent independent replication or more rigorous external certification. This system incentivizes the reporting of representative manufacturing yields and stability data over isolated champions, accelerating the research cycle by decoupling early-stage progress from slow certification queues while building a more robust, actionable map of technological readiness.

ideas.title: Improving Perovskite Stability via a Bio-inspired Self-Healing Encapsulation System
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original focus on encapsulation by drawing an analogy from biomedical engineering's stimuli-responsive drug delivery systems. The analogous solution uses engineered materials to release a healing agent on-demand in response to a specific environmental trigger. Adapted to perovskite solar cells, the idea is to develop a smart, bio-inspired encapsulation system. This system would consist of a primary barrier layer embedded with micro- or nano-capsules containing healing agents like perovskite precursors or passivation molecules. The capsule walls or the matrix material would be designed to degrade or become permeable only when exposed to specific degradation triggers inherent to perovskite failure modes—such as elevated humidity, oxygen, heat, or voltage-induced ion migration. Upon triggering, the localized release of the healing agent would autonomously repair defects, passivate interfaces, or replenish decomposed material, thereby actively combating degradation and significantly improving long-term operational stability to meet commercial requirements.

ideas.title: Dynamic Performance Rating for Shape-Conforming Photovoltaics
ideas.description: This research direction develops a dynamic performance rating framework for flexible and shape-conforming photovoltaics (PV). It replaces the fixed-area efficiency metric with a system where different cell technologies and form factors 'compete' based on their measured energy output under standardized, variable deployment scenarios (e.g., curved surfaces, moving substrates, partial shading). An adapted Elo/Glicko algorithm is then used to generate live, relative performance ratings from this competition data, creating a leaderboard that reflects which technologies adapt best to real-world, non-ideal conditions over time, rather than just peak lab efficiency.

ideas.title: Adapt a Clinical Trials Registry model for a dynamic, protocol-driven tandem cell efficiency database
ideas.description: The original idea is to create a dedicated tracking table for multi-junction and tandem solar cell efficiencies. Drawing an analogy from Medical Research & Clinical Trials, which solved the problem of fragmented, biased, and non-standardized reporting through centralized public registries like ClinicalTrials.gov. The analogous solution mandated pre-registration of study protocols and standardized outcome reporting to create a complete, transparent record. Adapting this, the research idea is to develop a dynamic database for tandem cell efficiencies that requires researchers to pre-register their detailed measurement and calibration protocols before submitting a record claim. This database would enforce standardized reporting criteria, archive all submitted data (including sub-record results), and provide a permanent, searchable ledger of experimental methods. This approach aims to combat efficiency inflation, improve inter-laboratory reproducibility, and create a more reliable historical resource for the photovoltaics research community by learning from the transparency frameworks of clinical science.
